* [Right-wing populism](/wiki/Right-wing_populism "Right-wing populism")[[a]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-Dominant-13) * [Conservatism](/wiki/Conservatism "Conservatism") ([US](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States"))[[13]](#cite_note-Smith-2021-14) **[Factions](/wiki/Factions_in_the_Republican_Party_(United_States) "Factions in the Republican Party (United States)")**: * [Christian right](/wiki/Christian_right "Christian right")[[14]](#cite_note-15) * [Right-libertarianism](/wiki/Right-libertarianism "Right-libertarianism") ([US](/wiki/Libertarianism_in_the_United_States "Libertarianism in the United States"))[[15]](#cite_note-Wilbur-2012-16)

* [International Democracy Union](/wiki/International_Democracy_Union "International Democracy Union")[[16]](#cite_note-17)   + [Asia Pacific Democracy Union](/wiki/Asia_Pacific_Democracy_Union "Asia Pacific Democracy Union")[[17]](#cite_note-18)   + [European Conservatives and Reformists Party](/wiki/European_Conservatives_and_Reformists_Party "European Conservatives and Reformists Party") (global partner)[[18]](#cite_note-19)

* [Republican Governance Group](/wiki/Republican_Governance_Group "Republican Governance Group") * [Republican Main Street Partnership](/wiki/Republican_Main_Street_Partnership#Affiliated_members_of_Congress "Republican Main Street Partnership") * [Republican Study Committee](/wiki/Republican_Study_Committee "Republican Study Committee") * [Freedom Caucus](/wiki/Freedom_Caucus "Freedom Caucus")

(**GOP**), is the major [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States")[*[discuss](/wiki/Talk:Republican_Party_(United_States) "Talk:Republican Party (United States)")*] and [right-wing](/wiki/Right-wing "Right-wing")[*[discuss](/wiki/Talk:Republican_Party_(United_States) "Talk:Republican Party (United States)")*] political party in the United States. It emerged as the main rival of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") in the 1850s, and the two parties have dominated [American politics](/wiki/American_politics "American politics") since then.

The **Republican Party**, commonly known as the **Grand Old Party** (**GOP**), is the major [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States")[*[discuss](/wiki/Talk:Republican_Party_(United_States) "Talk:Republican Party (United States)")*] and [right-wing](/wiki/Right-wing "Right-wing")[*[discuss](/wiki/Talk:Republican_Party_(United_States) "Talk:Republican Party (United States)")*] political party in the United States. It emerged as the main rival of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") in the 1850s, and the two parties have dominated [American politics](/wiki/American_politics "American politics") since then.

The Republican Party was founded in 1854 by [anti-slavery](/wiki/Anti-slavery "Anti-slavery") activists who opposed the [Kansas–Nebraska Act](/wiki/Kansas%E2%80%93Nebraska_Act "Kansas–Nebraska Act") and the expansion of [slavery into U.S. territories](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States"). It rapidly gained support in the [North](/wiki/Northern_United_States "Northern United States"), drawing in former [Whigs](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_States) "Whig Party (United States)"), [Free Soilers](/wiki/Free_Soil_Party "Free Soil Party"), and former [Know Nothings](/wiki/Know_Nothing "Know Nothing"). [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln")'s [election in 1860](/wiki/1860_United_States_presidential_election "1860 United States presidential election") led to the secession of Southern states and the outbreak of the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). Under Lincoln and a Republican-controlled Congress, the party led efforts to preserve the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)"), defeat the [Confederacy](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America"), and [abolish slavery](/wiki/End_of_slavery_in_the_United_States "End of slavery in the United States"). During the [Reconstruction era](/wiki/Reconstruction_era "Reconstruction era"), Republicans sought to extend [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights "Civil rights") protections to [freedmen](/wiki/Freedmen "Freedmen"), but by the late 1870s, the party shifted its focus toward business interests and [industrial expansion](/wiki/Industrialization_in_the_United_States "Industrialization in the United States"). In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, it dominated national politics, promoting [protective tariffs](/wiki/Protective_tariff "Protective tariff"), infrastructure development, and *[laissez-faire](/wiki/Laissez-faire "Laissez-faire")* economic policies, while navigating internal divisions between [progressive](/wiki/Progressive_Era "Progressive Era") and conservative [factions](/wiki/Factions_in_the_Republican_Party_(United_States) "Factions in the Republican Party (United States)"). The party's support declined during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), as the [New Deal coalition](/wiki/New_Deal_coalition "New Deal coalition") reshaped American politics. Republicans returned to national power with the [1952 election](/wiki/1952_United_States_presidential_election "1952 United States presidential election") of [Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower"), whose [moderate conservatism](/wiki/Rockefeller_Republican "Rockefeller Republican") reflected a pragmatic acceptance of many [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal")-era programs.

Following the [civil rights era](/wiki/Civil_rights_era "Civil rights era"), the Republican Party's use of the [Southern strategy](/wiki/Southern_strategy "Southern strategy") appealed to many white voters disaffected by Democratic support for civil rights. The [1980 election](/wiki/1980_United_States_presidential_election "1980 United States presidential election") of [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") realigned national politics, consolidating [a coalition](/wiki/Reagan_coalition "Reagan coalition") of [free market](/wiki/Free_market "Free market") advocates, [social conservatives](/wiki/Social_conservatives "Social conservatives") and foreign policy [hawks](/wiki/War_hawk "War hawk") under the Republican banner. Since 2009, a shift toward [right-wing populism](/wiki/Right-wing_populism "Right-wing populism")[[19]](#cite_note-RWP-21) culminated in the [2016 election](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election") of [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), whose leadership style and political agenda—often referred to as [Trumpism](/wiki/Trumpism "Trumpism")—reshaped the party's identity.[[9]](#cite_note-Ball_2024-9)[[10]](#cite_note-v075-10)[[11]](#cite_note-i277-11) By the early 2020s, the party had largely shifted towards [illiberalism](/wiki/Illiberal_democracy "Illiberal democracy"), with scholars noting [democratic backsliding in the U.S.](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding_in_the_United_States "Democratic backsliding in the United States") since [Trump's second presidency](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "Second presidency of Donald Trump") began in 2025.[[20]](#cite_note-Illiberalism-22)[[21]](#cite_note-:0-23)[[22]](#cite_note-Economist_12232025-24) A significant faction has begun to self-identify as [white nationalist](/wiki/White_nationalism "White nationalism").[[21]](#cite_note-:0-23)[[22]](#cite_note-Economist_12232025-24) In the 21st century, the Republican Party's strongest demographics are [rural voters](/wiki/Urban%E2%80%93rural_political_divide "Urban–rural political divide"), [White Southerners](/wiki/White_Southerners "White Southerners"), [evangelicals](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism"), men, [senior citizens](/wiki/Senior_citizens "Senior citizens"), and [voters](/wiki/Voter_turnout_in_United_States_presidential_elections "Voter turnout in United States presidential elections") without [college degrees](/wiki/Educational_attainment_in_the_United_States "Educational attainment in the United States").[[23]](#cite_note-gap_within_the_gender_gap-25)

On economic issues, the party has maintained a pro-capital attitude since its inception. It currently supports Trump's [mercantilist](/wiki/Mercantilism "Mercantilism") policies,[[24]](#cite_note-Trump's_aggressive_push-26)[[25]](#cite_note-Helleiner_2021-27) including [tariffs](/wiki/Tariffs_in_the_second_Trump_administration "Tariffs in the second Trump administration")[[26]](#cite_note-favor_Trump_tariffs2-28) on imports on all countries at the highest rates in the world[[27]](#cite_note-smashing_global_trade_norms-29)[[28]](#cite_note-Gilded_Age_vision-30) while opposing [globalization](/wiki/Economic_globalization "Economic globalization") and [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade"). It also supports low income taxes and deregulation while opposing [labor unions](/wiki/Labor_unions_in_the_United_States "Labor unions in the United States"), a [public health insurance option](/wiki/Public_health_insurance_option "Public health insurance option") and [single-payer healthcare](/wiki/Single-payer_healthcare "Single-payer healthcare").[[29]](#cite_note-New_Fusionism-31)[[30]](#cite_note-32) On social issues, it advocates for [restricting abortion](/wiki/Abortion_law_in_the_United_States_by_state "Abortion law in the United States by state")[[31]](#cite_note-33) and supports some [tough-on-crime](/wiki/Law_and_order_(politics) "Law and order (politics)") policies such as [capital punishment](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_the_United_States "Capital punishment in the United States") but is divided on the prohibition of [recreational drug use](/wiki/Recreational_drug_use "Recreational drug use").[[32]](#cite_note-34) It promotes gun ownership, [easing gun restrictions](/wiki/Gun_law_in_the_United_States "Gun law in the United States"),[[33]](#cite_note-35) and opposes [transgender rights](/wiki/Transgender_rights "Transgender rights").[[34]](#cite_note-36) Views on immigration within the party vary; it generally supports limited legal immigration but is strongly in favor of crackdowns on [illegal immigration](/wiki/Illegal_immigration_to_the_United_States "Illegal immigration to the United States") and the [deportation](/wiki/Deportation_from_the_United_States "Deportation from the United States") of those without permanent legal status, such as [undocumented immigrants](/wiki/Illegal_immigration "Illegal immigration") and those with [temporary protected status](/wiki/Temporary_protected_status "Temporary protected status"). In foreign policy, the party mostly supports U.S. aid to Israel but is divided on [aid to Ukraine](/wiki/Military_aid_to_Ukraine_during_the_Russo-Ukrainian_war "Military aid to Ukraine during the Russo-Ukrainian war")[[35]](#cite_note-Riccardi-37) and improving [relations with Russia](/wiki/Russia%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Russia–United States relations").

## History

Main article: [History of the Republican Party (United States)](/wiki/History_of_the_Republican_Party_(United_States) "History of the Republican Party (United States)")

### 1850s to 1930s

In 1854, the Republican Party began as an antislavery party to combat the expansion of slavery into western territories after the passing of the [Kansas–Nebraska Act](/wiki/Kansas%E2%80%93Nebraska_Act "Kansas–Nebraska Act") by the Democrats under [Stephan Douglas](/wiki/Stephen_A._Douglas "Stephen A. Douglas"). The party was formed in the Northern and Border states, but not the South. It rapidly gained support in the [North](/wiki/Northern_United_States "Northern United States"), drawing in former [Whigs](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_States) "Whig Party (United States)") and [Free Soil Democrats](/wiki/Free_Soil_Party "Free Soil Party"). Under the [Presidency of Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Presidency_of_Abraham_Lincoln "Presidency of Abraham Lincoln") it led the successful war effort against the Confederacy.[[36]](#cite_note-38)

By 1865 it encompassed northern Protestants, factory workers, professionals, businessmen, prosperous farmers, and also black former slaves. It also included a minority of white Southerners who had opposed the Confederacy—men ridiculed by Democrats as "Scalawags". The great majority of white southerners were Democrats, as were the great majority of Irish Catholics and German Catholics. While both parties adopted [pro-business](/wiki/Pro-business "Pro-business") policies in the 19th century, the early GOP was distinguished by its support for the [national banking system](/wiki/Banking_in_the_United_States "Banking in the United States"), the [gold standard](/wiki/Gold_standard "Gold standard"), [railroads](/wiki/Rail_transportation_in_the_United_States "Rail transportation in the United States"), and [high tariffs](/wiki/Protectionism "Protectionism").[[37]](#cite_note-39)

The Republican Party largely dominated the national political scene until 1932. However it split in 1912, as former president [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") formed the liberal [Progressive Party](/wiki/Bull_Moose_Party "Bull Moose Party") to oppose the conservatives under [William Howard Taft](/wiki/William_Howard_Taft "William Howard Taft"). The split allowed Democrats under [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") to win [in 1912](/wiki/1912_United_States_presidential_election "1912 United States presidential election"). The GOP lost its congressional majorities during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") (1929–1940). Under [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") and [Harry S Truman](/wiki/Harry_S_Truman "Harry S Truman"), the Democrats formed a winning [New Deal coalition](/wiki/New_Deal_coalition "New Deal coalition") that was dominant from 1932 through 1952.[[38]](#cite_note-40)

### Shift rightward

After the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964"), the [Voting Rights Act of 1965](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act_of_1965 "Voting Rights Act of 1965"), and the [Southern strategy](/wiki/Southern_strategy "Southern strategy"), the party's core base shifted with the Southern states becoming more reliably Republican in presidential politics and the Northeastern states becoming more reliably Democratic. White voters increasingly identified with the Republican Party after the 1960s.[[39]](#cite_note-41) Following the Supreme Court's 1973 decision in *[Roe v. Wade](/wiki/Roe_v._Wade "Roe v. Wade")*, the Republican Party opposed abortion in its party platform and grew its support among [evangelicals](/wiki/Evangelicalism_in_the_United_States "Evangelicalism in the United States").[[40]](#cite_note-The_Great_Divide:_Religious_and_Cultural_Conflict_in_American_Party_Politics-42) The Republican Party won five of the six presidential elections from 1968 to 1988. Two-term President [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan"), who held office from 1981 to 1989, was a transformative party leader. His [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") policies called for reduced social [government spending](/wiki/Government_spending "Government spending") and regulation, increased military spending, [lower taxes](/wiki/Tax_cut "Tax cut"), and a strong [anti-Soviet](/wiki/Anti-Sovietism "Anti-Sovietism") foreign policy. Reagan's influence upon the party persisted into the 21st century.

Since the 1990s, the party's support has chiefly come from the South, the [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains"), the [Mountain States](/wiki/Mountain_States "Mountain States"), and [rural areas](/wiki/Rural_areas_in_the_United_States "Rural areas in the United States") in the North.[[41]](#cite_note-auto3-43)[[42]](#cite_note-44)

### Trump era

Main articles: [First presidency of Donald Trump](/wiki/First_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "First presidency of Donald Trump"), [Second presidency of Donald Trump](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "Second presidency of Donald Trump"), and [Trumpism](/wiki/Trumpism "Trumpism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Official_Presidential_Portrait_of_President_Donald_J._Trump_%282025%29.jpg/250px-Official_Presidential_Portrait_of_President_Donald_J._Trump_%282025%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_Presidential_Portrait_of_President_Donald_J._Trump_(2025).jpg)

[Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), the 45th (2017–2021) and 47th (since 2025) president

In the [2016 presidential election](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election"), Republican nominee [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") defeated Democratic nominee [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton"). The result was unexpected; polls leading up to the election showed Clinton leading the race.[[43]](#cite_note-45) Trump's victory was fueled by narrow victories in three states—[Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), and [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin")—that had been part of the [Democratic blue wall](/wiki/Blue_wall_(United_States) "Blue wall (United States)") for decades.[[44]](#cite_note-elites-46) It was attributed to strong support amongst working-class white voters, who felt dismissed and disrespected by the political establishment.[[45]](#cite_note-campani-47)[[46]](#cite_note-48) Trump became popular with them by abandoning Republican establishment orthodoxy in favor of a broader nationalist message.[[44]](#cite_note-elites-46) His election accelerated the Republican Party's shift towards right-wing populism and resulted in decreasing influence among its conservative factions.[[19]](#cite_note-RWP-21)

After the [2016 elections](/wiki/2016_United_States_elections "2016 United States elections"), Republicans [maintained their majority in the Senate](/wiki/2016_United_States_Senate_elections "2016 United States Senate elections"), [the House](/wiki/2016_United_States_House_of_Representatives_elections "2016 United States House of Representatives elections"), and [governorships](/wiki/2016_United_States_gubernatorial_elections "2016 United States gubernatorial elections"), and wielded newly acquired executive power with Trump's election. The Republican Party controlled 69 of 99 state legislative chambers in 2017, the most it had held in history.[[47]](#cite_note-49) The Party also held 33 governorships,[[48]](#cite_note-50) the most it had held since 1922.[[49]](#cite_note-51) The party had total control of government in 25 states,[[50]](#cite_note-52)[[51]](#cite_note-53) the most since 1952.[[52]](#cite_note-54) The opposing Democratic Party held full control of only five states in 2017.[[53]](#cite_note-55) In the [2018 elections](/wiki/2018_United_States_elections "2018 United States elections"), Republicans lost control of the House, but strengthened their hold on the Senate.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

Over the course of his presidency, Trump appointed three justices to the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States").[[55]](#cite_note-57) Trump lost the [2020 presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election") to [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") but refused to concede the race, claiming widespread electoral fraud and [attempting to overturn the results](/wiki/Attempts_to_overturn_the_2020_United_States_presidential_election "Attempts to overturn the 2020 United States presidential election"). On January 6, 2021, the [United States Capitol was attacked by Trump supporters](/wiki/January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack "January 6 United States Capitol attack") following a rally at which Trump spoke. After the attack, the House [impeached Trump for a second time](/wiki/Second_impeachment_of_Donald_Trump "Second impeachment of Donald Trump") on the charge of [incitement of insurrection](/wiki/Sedition "Sedition"), making him the only federal officeholder to be impeached twice; however, he was not removed from office.[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59) The Senate [acquitted him](/wiki/Second_impeachment_trial_of_Donald_Trump "Second impeachment trial of Donald Trump") in February 2021, after he had already left office.[[58]](#cite_note-60) Following the 2020 election, [election denial](/wiki/Election_denial_movement_in_the_United_States "Election denial movement in the United States") became increasingly mainstream in the party,[[59]](#cite_note-c990-61) with the majority of Republican candidates in 2022 being election deniers.[[60]](#cite_note-t387-62) The party also made [efforts to restrict voting](/wiki/Republican_efforts_to_restrict_voting_following_the_2020_presidential_election "Republican efforts to restrict voting following the 2020 presidential election") based on false claims of fraud.[[61]](#cite_note-63)[[62]](#cite_note-64) By the early 2020s, the Republican Party had largely shifted towards [illiberalism](/wiki/Illiberal_democracy "Illiberal democracy"),[[20]](#cite_note-Illiberalism-22) with research conducted by the [V-Dem Institute](/wiki/V-Dem_Institute "V-Dem Institute") concluding that it was more similar to Europe's most right-wing parties such as [Law and Justice](/wiki/Law_and_Justice "Law and Justice") in Poland or [Fidesz](/wiki/Fidesz "Fidesz") in Hungary.[[63]](#cite_note-Economist_10312020-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66)

Trump [easily won the nomination](/wiki/2024_Republican_Party_presidential_primaries "2024 Republican Party presidential primaries") to be the party's [candidate again in 2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"), marking the third straight election of him being the GOP nominee.[[65]](#cite_note-67) Trump achieved victory against Vice President [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris"). He won both the [electoral college](/wiki/United_States_electoral_college "United States electoral college") and a [plurality](/wiki/Plurality_(voting) "Plurality (voting)") of the popular vote, becoming the first Republican to do so since George W. Bush in 2004, and improving his vote share among [working class](/wiki/Working_class_in_the_United_States "Working class in the United States") voters, particularly among young men, those without college degrees, and [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") voters.[[66]](#cite_note-Lange-2024-68)

### Current status

As of 2026, the GOP holds the presidency, and majorities in both the [U.S. House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") and [U.S. Senate](/wiki/U.S._Senate "U.S. Senate"), giving them a federal [government trifecta](/wiki/Government_trifecta "Government trifecta"). It also holds 26 [state governorships](/wiki/List_of_United_States_governors "List of United States governors"), 28 [state legislatures](/wiki/List_of_United_States_state_legislatures "List of United States state legislatures"), and 23 state government trifectas. Six of the nine current [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court") justices were appointed by Republican presidents. There have been 19 Republicans who have served as president, the most from any one political party;[[67]](#cite_note-69) the most recent being current president [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), who became the 47th president on January 20, 2025. Trump also served as the 45th president from 2017 to 2021.[[68]](#cite_note-s635-70)

During Trump's second term, the GOP has been partially divided on a number of key policies, including [tariffs](/wiki/Tariffs_in_the_second_Trump_administration "Tariffs in the second Trump administration") and support for Ukraine against Russia, according to [Congress.net](https://congress.net/) and [Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/).[[69]](#cite_note-71) [[70]](#cite_note-72)

## Name and symbols

The Republican Party's founding members chose its name as homage to the values of [republicanism](/wiki/Republicanism_in_the_United_States "Republicanism in the United States") promoted by the Democratic-Republican Party, which its founder, Thomas Jefferson, called the "Republican Party".[[71]](#cite_note-Rutland-73) The idea for the name came from an editorial by the party's leading publicist, [Horace Greeley](/wiki/Horace_Greeley "Horace Greeley"), who called for "some simple name like 'Republican' [that] would more fitly designate those who had united to restore the Union to its true mission of champion and promulgator of Liberty rather than propagandist of slavery".[[72]](#cite_note-ushistory-74) The name was selected as it "connected voters with the original political organization of Thomas Jefferson in the 1790s, the Democratic-Republican Party".[[73]](#cite_note-Gould2003p14-75) "Republican" has a variety of meanings around the world, and the Republican Party has evolved such that the meanings no longer always align.[[74]](#cite_note-Republican_Party_|_political_party-76)[[75]](#cite_note-77)

The term "Grand Old Party" is a traditional nickname for the Republican Party, and the abbreviation "GOP" is a commonly used designation. The term originated in 1875 in the *[Congressional Record](/wiki/Congressional_Record "Congressional Record")*, referring to the party associated with the successful military defense of the Union as "this gallant old party". The following year in an article in the *[Cincinnati Commercial](/wiki/Cincinnati_Commercial "Cincinnati Commercial")*, the term was modified to "grand old party". The first use of the abbreviation is dated 1884.[[76]](#cite_note-78)

The traditional mascot of the party is the elephant. A political cartoon by [Thomas Nast](/wiki/Thomas_Nast "Thomas Nast"), published in *[Harper's Weekly](/wiki/Harper%27s_Weekly "Harper's Weekly")* on November 7, 1874, is considered the first important use of the symbol.[[77]](#cite_note-harpweek-79) The cartoon was published during the debate over [possibly run for third term](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant#Third_term_attempt "Ulysses S. Grant") from President [Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant"). It draws imagery and text from the [Aesop](/wiki/Aesop "Aesop") fable "[The Ass in the Lion's Skin](/wiki/The_Ass_in_the_Lion%27s_Skin "The Ass in the Lion's Skin")", combined with rumors of animals escaping from the [Central Park Zoo](/wiki/Central_Park_Zoo "Central Park Zoo").
An alternate symbol of the Republican Party in states such as Indiana, New York, and Ohio is the bald eagle as opposed to the Democratic rooster or the Democratic five-pointed star.[[78]](#cite_note-80)[[79]](#cite_note-81) In [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky"), the [log cabin](/wiki/Log_cabin "Log cabin") is a symbol of the Republican Party.[[80]](#cite_note-82)

Traditionally the party had no consistent color identity.[[81]](#cite_note-83)[[82]](#cite_note-84)[[83]](#cite_note-85) After the 2000 presidential election, the color red became [politically associated](/wiki/Political_colour "Political colour") with Republicans. During and after the election, the major broadcast networks used the same color scheme for the electoral map: states won by Republican nominee George W. Bush were colored red and states won by Democratic nominee [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore") were colored blue. Due to the weeks-long [dispute over the election results](/wiki/2000_United_States_presidential_election_recount_in_Florida "2000 United States presidential election recount in Florida"), these color associations became firmly ingrained, persisting in subsequent years. Although the assignment of colors to political parties is unofficial and informal, the media has come to represent the respective political parties using these colors. The party and its candidates have also come to embrace the color red.[[84]](#cite_note-86)
