## Factions

Main article: [Factions in the Republican Party (United States)](/wiki/Factions_in_the_Republican_Party_(United_States) "Factions in the Republican Party (United States)")

### Civil War and Reconstruction era

Main article: [Radical Republicans](/wiki/Radical_Republicans "Radical Republicans")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Thaddeus_Stevens_-_Brady-Handy-crop.jpg/250px-Thaddeus_Stevens_-_Brady-Handy-crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thaddeus_Stevens_-_Brady-Handy-crop.jpg)

U.S. representative [Thaddeus Stevens](/wiki/Thaddeus_Stevens "Thaddeus Stevens"), considered a leader of the Radical Republicans, was a fierce opponent of slavery and discrimination against African Americans.

The Radical Republicans were a major factor in the party from its inception in 1854 until the end of the [Reconstruction Era](/wiki/Reconstruction_Era "Reconstruction Era") in 1877. Like the moderate Republicans the radicals strongly opposed the expansion of [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States"). Unlike the moderates they were hard-line [abolitionists](/wiki/Abolitionism "Abolitionism") calling for the end of slavery in the South. By 1865 Both factions advocated equal rights for the [freedmen](/wiki/Freedmen "Freedmen"). Radicals were heavily influenced by religious ideals and [evangelical Christianity](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism").[[85]](#cite_note-Howard2015-87) Radical Republicans pressed for abolition as a major war aim and they opposed the moderate Reconstruction plans of Abraham Lincoln as both too lenient on the [Confederates](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") and not going far enough to help former slaves. After the war's end and Lincoln's assassination, the Radicals clashed with [Andrew Johnson](/wiki/Andrew_Johnson "Andrew Johnson") over Reconstruction policy. Radicals led efforts to establish civil rights for former slaves and fully implement emancipation, pushing the [Fourteenth Amendment](/wiki/Fourteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") for statutory protections through [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"). They opposed allowing ex-[Confederate](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") officers to retake political power in the [Southern U.S.](/wiki/Southern_U.S. "Southern U.S."), and emphasized liberty, equality, and the [Fifteenth Amendment](/wiki/Fifteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fifteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") which provided [voting rights](/wiki/Voting_rights "Voting rights") for the [freedmen](/wiki/Freedman#United_States "Freedman"). Many later became [Stalwarts](/wiki/Stalwarts "Stalwarts"), who supported machine politics.

[Moderate Republicans](/wiki/Moderate_Republicans_(Reconstruction_era) "Moderate Republicans (Reconstruction era)") were known for their loyal support of President [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln")'s war policies and expressed antipathy towards the more militant stances advocated by the Radical Republicans. In contrast to Radicals, Moderate Republicans were less enthusiastic on the issue of Black suffrage even while embracing civil equality and the expansive federal authority observed throughout the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). They were also skeptical of the lenient, conciliatory Reconstruction policies of President Andrew Johnson. Members of the Moderate Republicans comprised in part of the previous Radical Republicans who became disenchanted with the alleged corruption of the latter faction. They generally opposed efforts by Radical Republicans to rebuild the Southern U.S. under an economically mobile, [free-market](/wiki/Free_market "Free market") system.[[86]](#cite_note-88)

### 20th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Goldwater-Reagan_in_1964.jpg/250px-Goldwater-Reagan_in_1964.jpg)](/wiki/File:Goldwater-Reagan_in_1964.jpg)

[Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") speaks in support of Republican presidential candidate [Barry Goldwater](/wiki/Barry_Goldwater "Barry Goldwater") during the [1964 presidential campaign](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election").

The 20th century saw the Republican party split into an [Old Right](/wiki/Old_Right_(United_States) "Old Right (United States)") and a moderate-liberal faction in the Northeast that eventually became known as [Rockefeller Republicans](/wiki/Rockefeller_Republicans "Rockefeller Republicans"). Opposition to Roosevelt's [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") saw the formation of the [conservative coalition](/wiki/Conservative_coalition "Conservative coalition").[[87]](#cite_note-Bowen-89) The 1950s saw [fusionism](/wiki/Fusionism "Fusionism") of traditionalist and social conservatism and right-libertarianism,[[29]](#cite_note-New_Fusionism-31) along with the rise of the [First New Right](/wiki/New_Right#United_States "New Right") to be followed in 1964 with a more populist [Second New Right](/wiki/New_Right#Second_New_Right "New Right").[[88]](#cite_note-90)

The rise of the [Reagan coalition](/wiki/Reagan_coalition "Reagan coalition") in the 1980s began what has been called the [Reagan era](/wiki/Reagan_era "Reagan era"). Reagan's rise displaced the liberal-moderate faction of the GOP and established Reagan-style conservatism as the prevailing ideological faction of the Party for the next thirty years, until the rise of the [right-wing populist](/wiki/Right-wing_populism "Right-wing populism") faction.[[13]](#cite_note-Smith-2021-14)[[89]](#cite_note-Ward_08-26-22-91) Reagan conservatives generally supported policies that favored [limited government](/wiki/Limited_government "Limited government"), [individualism](/wiki/Individualism "Individualism"), traditionalism, [republicanism](/wiki/Republicanism_in_the_United_States "Republicanism in the United States"), and limited [federal governmental](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") power [in relation](/wiki/States%27_rights "States' rights") to [the states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state").[[90]](#cite_note-political-ideology-today-92)

### 21st century

See also: [Neoconservatism](/wiki/Neoconservatism "Neoconservatism"), [Tea Party movement](/wiki/Tea_Party_movement "Tea Party movement"), [Right-wing populism](/wiki/Right-wing_populism "Right-wing populism"), and [Trumpism](/wiki/Trumpism "Trumpism")

Republicans began the 21st century with the election of [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") in the [2000 United States presidential election](/wiki/2000_United_States_presidential_election "2000 United States presidential election") and saw the peak of a neoconservative faction that held significant influence over the initial American response to the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") through the [war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror").[[91]](#cite_note-Rathburn_2008-93) The election of [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") saw the formation of the Tea Party movement in 2009 that coincided with a global rise in right-wing populist movements from the 2010s to 2020s.[[92]](#cite_note-Isaac2017-94) The global rise in right-wing populism has been attributed to factors including economic insecurity due to [financialization](/wiki/Financialization "Financialization"),[[93]](#cite_note-95) a decline in organized religion, backlash to globalization, and [migrant crises](/wiki/Migrant_crisis "Migrant crisis").[[94]](#cite_note-Fallen_Behind-96)[[95]](#cite_note-Maxwell_2019-97)

Right-wing populism became an increasingly dominant ideological faction within the GOP throughout the 2010s and helped lead to the election of [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") in 2016.[[45]](#cite_note-campani-47) Starting in the 1970s and accelerating in the 2000s, American right-wing interest groups invested heavily in external mobilization vehicles that led to the organizational weakening of the GOP establishment. The outsized role of conservative media, in particular [Fox News](/wiki/Fox_News "Fox News"), led to it being followed and trusted more by the Republican base over traditional party elites. The depletion of organizational capacity partly led to Trump's victory in the Republican primaries against the wishes of a very weak party establishment and traditional power brokers.[[96]](#cite_note-Gidron-2019-98): 27–28 Trump's election exacerbated internal schisms within the GOP,[[96]](#cite_note-Gidron-2019-98): 18 and saw the GOP move from a center coalition of moderates and conservatives to a solidly right-wing party hostile to liberal views and any deviations from the party line.[[97]](#cite_note-99)

The Party has since faced intense factionalism.[[98]](#cite_note-100)[[99]](#cite_note-101) These factions are particularly apparent in the [U.S. House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), where three Republican House leaders ([Eric Cantor](/wiki/Eric_Cantor "Eric Cantor"), [John Boehner](/wiki/John_Boehner "John Boehner"), and [Kevin McCarthy](/wiki/Kevin_McCarthy "Kevin McCarthy")) have been ousted since 2009.[[100]](#cite_note-McCarthy_2009-102)[[101]](#cite_note-103)[[102]](#cite_note-newyorker-104)[[103]](#cite_note-resign1-105)[[104]](#cite_note-106)[[105]](#cite_note-107) All three of the top Republican elected officials during Trump's first term (Vice President, Speaker of the House, and Senate Republican leader) were ousted or stepped down by Trump's second term.

The party's [establishment](/wiki/The_Establishment#United_States "The Establishment") conservative faction has lost all of its influence.[[106]](#cite_note-Biebricher-2023-108)[[107]](#cite_note-Arhin-2023-109)[[108]](#cite_note-Punchbowl_Old_GOP-110)[[109]](#cite_note-Price_of_Power-111) Many conservatives critical of the Trumpist faction have also lost influence within the party, with no former Republican presidential or vice presidential nominees attending the [2024 Republican National Convention](/wiki/2024_Republican_National_Convention "2024 Republican National Convention").[[110]](#cite_note-Not_Coming_to_Milwaukee-112)[[111]](#cite_note-washingtonpost.com-113)

The victory of Trump in the 2024 presidential election saw the party increasingly shift towards Trumpism,[[112]](#cite_note-Cohn_12252024-114)[[9]](#cite_note-Ball_2024-9) and party criticism of Trump was described as being muted to non-existent. *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* described it as a "hostile takeover",[[113]](#cite_note-Swan_12122024-115) and a victory of right-wing populism over the old conservative establishment.[[112]](#cite_note-Cohn_12252024-114)[[109]](#cite_note-Price_of_Power-111)[[114]](#cite_note-Cliffe_2023-116) Polling found that 53% of Republican voters saw loyalty to Trump as central to their political identity and what it means to be a Republican.[[115]](#cite_note-117) During Trump's second presidency, Republican members of Congress were described by political commentators and news media as largely submissive to Trump, letting him dictate policies without pushback.[[116]](#cite_note-118)[[117]](#cite_note-119)

#### Right-wing populists and Trumpists

Main articles: [Right-wing populism](/wiki/Right-wing_populism "Right-wing populism") and [Trumpism](/wiki/Trumpism "Trumpism")

See also: [Radical right (United States)](/wiki/Radical_right_(United_States) "Radical right (United States)"), [National conservatism](/wiki/National_conservatism "National conservatism"), and [Freedom Caucus](/wiki/Freedom_Caucus "Freedom Caucus")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/January_2025_Official_Vice_Presidential_Portrait_of_JD_Vance.jpg/250px-January_2025_Official_Vice_Presidential_Portrait_of_JD_Vance.jpg)](/wiki/File:January_2025_Official_Vice_Presidential_Portrait_of_JD_Vance.jpg)

[JD Vance](/wiki/JD_Vance "JD Vance"), Donald Trump's vice president during Trump's second term. Initially critical of Trump, he became a staunch advocate of Trumpism later in Trump's first term and has been described as a right-wing populist.[[118]](#cite_note-120)

Right-wing populism is the dominant political faction of the GOP.[[12]](#cite_note-Dominant-13) Sometimes referred to as the [MAGA](/wiki/Make_America_Great_Again "Make America Great Again") or "[America First](/wiki/America_First "America First")" movement,[[119]](#cite_note-University_of_Washington_2021-121)[[120]](#cite_note-Gabbatt_Smith_2023-122) Republican populists have been described as consisting of a range of right-wing ideologies including but not limited to right-wing populism,[[45]](#cite_note-campani-47)[[121]](#cite_note-123)[[122]](#cite_note-Cassidy-124) national conservatism,[[123]](#cite_note-Economist_Feb152024-125) [neo-nationalism](/wiki/Neo-nationalism "Neo-nationalism"),[[124]](#cite_note-Zhou_12/8/2022-126)[[125]](#cite_note-Maga_mindset-127) [mercantilism](/wiki/Mercantilism "Mercantilism"),[[25]](#cite_note-Helleiner_2021-27) and Trumpism.[[9]](#cite_note-Ball_2024-9)[[126]](#cite_note-Katzenstein2019-128)[[10]](#cite_note-v075-10)[[127]](#cite_note-d419-129)[[11]](#cite_note-i277-11)[[128]](#cite_note-b702-130) Trump has been described as one of many nationalist leaders, including [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") of Russia, [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") of China, [Recep Tayyip Erdogan](/wiki/Recep_Tayyip_Erdogan "Recep Tayyip Erdogan") of Turkey, [Narendra Modi](/wiki/Narendra_Modi "Narendra Modi") of India, [Mohammed bin Salman](/wiki/Mohammed_bin_Salman "Mohammed bin Salman") of Saudi Arabia, [Viktor Orbán](/wiki/Viktor_Orb%C3%A1n "Viktor Orbán") of Hungary and [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu") of Israel.[[129]](#cite_note-The_World_Trump_Wants-131)[[130]](#cite_note-132)[[131]](#cite_note-133)[[132]](#cite_note-134)

The Republican Party's right-wing populist movements emerged in concurrence with a global increase in populist movements in the 2010s and 2020s,[[92]](#cite_note-Isaac2017-94)[[95]](#cite_note-Maxwell_2019-97) coupled with entrenchment and increased partisanship within the party since 2010.[[133]](#cite_note-Lowndes_2021_q431-135) This included the rise of the [Tea Party movement](/wiki/Tea_Party_movement "Tea Party movement"), which has also been described as far-right.[[134]](#cite_note-Blum_pp._88–109-136) This faction gained further dominance in the GOP during [Joe Biden's presidency](/wiki/Presidency_of_Joe_Biden "Presidency of Joe Biden") (2021–2025), including in the aftermath of the [2021–2023 inflation surge](/wiki/2021%E2%80%932023_inflation_surge "2021–2023 inflation surge") and [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[135]](#cite_note-No_Longer_True-137)

Businessman [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk"), the wealthiest individual in the world, is a notable proponent of right-wing populism.[[136]](#cite_note-138) Since acquiring Twitter in 2022, Musk has shared far-right misinformation[[137]](#cite_note-139)[[138]](#cite_note-140)[[139]](#cite_note-141) and numerous [conspiracy theories](/wiki/Conspiracy_theories "Conspiracy theories"),[[140]](#cite_note-142)[[141]](#cite_note-143)[[142]](#cite_note-144) and his views are described as right-wing to far-right.[[143]](#cite_note-145)[[144]](#cite_note-146)[[145]](#cite_note-147)[[146]](#cite_note-148)[[147]](#cite_note-149) However, Musk has also been described as in conflict with the populist wing of the party on some issues, particularly [legal immigration](/wiki/Legal_immigration "Legal immigration"), [government spending](/wiki/Government_spending "Government spending"), [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade"), [judicial reforms](/wiki/Judicial_reform "Judicial reform") and [relations with China](/wiki/US_-_China_relations "US - China relations").[[148]](#cite_note-150)[[149]](#cite_note-151)[[150]](#cite_note-152)[[151]](#cite_note-153)[[152]](#cite_note-154)[[153]](#cite_note-BBBmiller-155)[[154]](#cite_note-156)

According to political scientists Matt Grossmann and David A. Hopkins, the Republican Party's gains among white voters without college degrees and corresponding losses among white voters with college degrees contributed to the rise of right-wing populism.[[155]](#cite_note-cambridge.org-157) Until 2016, white voters with college degrees were a Republican-leaning group, but have since become a Democratic-leaning group.[[156]](#cite_note-Harry_Enten-158) In the [2020 presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election"), [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") became the first Democratic president to win a majority of white voters with college degrees (51–48%) since [1964](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election"), while Trump won white voters without college degrees 67–32%.[[157]](#cite_note-cnn2020PresidentialResults-159)[[158]](#cite_note-auto-160)[[159]](#cite_note-Polarization_by_education-161) In the [2024 presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"), Trump won white men without college degrees in a landslide (69-29%), and lost white women with college degrees (41-58%).[[23]](#cite_note-gap_within_the_gender_gap-25)

Right-wing populism has broad appeal across income and wealth,[[160]](#cite_note-Nate_Silver-162) and is extremely polarized with respect to educational attainment among White voters.[[161]](#cite_note-163) According to a 2017 study, agreement with Trump on social issues, rather than economic pressure, increased support for Trump among White voters without college degrees. White voters without college degrees who were economically struggling were more likely to vote for Democrats and support the Democratic Party's economic agenda.[[162]](#cite_note-164)[[163]](#cite_note-165) Right-wing populism has appeal to [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") and [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans") voters,[[164]](#cite_note-166)[[165]](#cite_note-asian_americans_5_points-167) but has little appeal to African American voters.[[166]](#cite_note-Berman-168)

According to historian [Gary Gerstle](/wiki/Gary_Gerstle "Gary Gerstle"), Trumpism gained support in opposition to [neoliberalism](/wiki/Neoliberalism "Neoliberalism"),[[167]](#cite_note-Gerstle2022-169)[[129]](#cite_note-The_World_Trump_Wants-131) including opposition to [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade"),[[168]](#cite_note-Populism_and_Trade-170)[[169]](#cite_note-trade_policies_back-171) [immigration](/wiki/Opposition_to_immigration "Opposition to immigration"), [globalization](/wiki/Globalization#Economic_globalization "Globalization"),[[135]](#cite_note-No_Longer_True-137)[[125]](#cite_note-Maga_mindset-127) and [internationalism](/wiki/Liberal_internationalism "Liberal internationalism").[[95]](#cite_note-Maxwell_2019-97) Trump won the 2016 and 2024 presidential elections by winning states in the [Rust Belt](/wiki/Rust_Belt "Rust Belt") that had suffered from [population decline](/wiki/Population_decline "Population decline") and deindustrialization.[[170]](#cite_note-Revolt_of_the_Rust_Belt-172)[[94]](#cite_note-Fallen_Behind-96) Compared to other Republicans, the populist faction is more likely to oppose [legal immigration](/wiki/Immigration "Immigration"),[[171]](#cite_note-Baker-2020-173) free trade,[[168]](#cite_note-Populism_and_Trade-170) [neoconservatism](/wiki/Neoconservatism "Neoconservatism"),[[172]](#cite_note-174) and [environmental protection laws](/wiki/Environmentalism "Environmentalism").[[173]](#cite_note-175) It has been described as featuring [anti-intellectualism](/wiki/Anti-intellectualism "Anti-intellectualism") and overtly racial appeals.[[174]](#cite_note-Winberg_2017-176)

In international relations, populists typically support U.S. aid to Israel but not to Ukraine,[[175]](#cite_note-Falk_2023_t804-177)[[35]](#cite_note-Riccardi-37) though since 2025, a growing element is critical of aid to Israel as well.[[176]](#cite_note-178) They are generally supportive of improving [relations with Russia](/wiki/Russia%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Russia–United States relations"),[[177]](#cite_note-Lillis-179)[[178]](#cite_note-Ball-180)[[179]](#cite_note-Jonathan-181)[[180]](#cite_note-Jimison-182) and favor an [isolationist](/wiki/Isolationism "Isolationism") "[America First](/wiki/America_First "America First")" foreign policy agenda.[[181]](#cite_note-Lange-183)[[182]](#cite_note-New_York_Times-184)[[183]](#cite_note-Baker-185)[[184]](#cite_note-Cohn2023-186) This faction has been described as closer to that of Vladimir Putin's Russia and Recep Tayyip Erdogan's Turkey than Western Europe and the [Anglosphere](/wiki/Anglosphere "Anglosphere") in terms of positions on international cooperation, support for an autocratic leadership style, and trust in institutions.[[125]](#cite_note-Maga_mindset-127) This faction takes nationalist and [irredentist](/wiki/Irredentist "Irredentist") views towards other countries in North America, advocating for U.S. territorial expansion to include [Canada](/wiki/Movements_for_the_annexation_of_Canada_to_the_United_States#Proposals_to_annex_Canada_by_Donald_Trump "Movements for the annexation of Canada to the United States"), [Greenland](/wiki/Proposed_United_States_acquisition_of_Greenland "Proposed United States acquisition of Greenland") and the [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal"), the renaming of the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"), and potential military action on Mexican soil.[[185]](#cite_note-187)[[186]](#cite_note-188)[[187]](#cite_note-189)[[188]](#cite_note-190)

The party's far-right faction includes members of the Freedom Caucus.[[189]](#cite_note-Chatelain_2023_d086-191)[[190]](#cite_note-NBC4_Washington_2023_e016-192)[[191]](#cite_note-Hulse_2023_y458-193)[[192]](#cite_note-Mascaro_Freking_Amiri_2023_a302-194) They generally reject compromise within the party and with the [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"),[[193]](#cite_note-Collinson_2023_n804-195)[[194]](#cite_note-Rocha_2023_k444-196) and are willing to oust fellow Republican office holders they deem to be too moderate.[[195]](#cite_note-Macpherson_2021_r371-197)[[196]](#cite_note-Times-Herald.com_2023_x358-198) According to sociologist [Joe Feagin](/wiki/Joe_Feagin "Joe Feagin"), political polarization by racially extremist Republicans as well as their increased attention from conservative media has perpetuated the near extinction of moderate Republicans and created legislative paralysis at numerous government levels in the last few decades.[[197]](#cite_note-199)[[198]](#cite_note-200)

[Julia Azari](/wiki/Julia_Azari "Julia Azari"), an associate professor of political science at [Marquette University](/wiki/Marquette_University "Marquette University"), noted that not all populist Republicans are public supporters of Donald Trump, and that some Republicans such as [Glenn Youngkin](/wiki/Glenn_Youngkin "Glenn Youngkin"), a former [Governor of Virginia](/wiki/Governor_of_Virginia "Governor of Virginia"), endorse Trump policies while distancing themselves from Trump as a person.[[199]](#cite_note-j483-201)[[200]](#cite_note-Youngkin-202) The continued dominance of Trump within the GOP has limited the success of this strategy.[[201]](#cite_note-i073-203)[[202]](#cite_note-204)[[203]](#cite_note-c660-205) In 2024, Trump led a takeover of the [Republican National Committee](/wiki/Republican_National_Committee "Republican National Committee").[[204]](#cite_note-206)

A *[FiveThirtyEight](/wiki/FiveThirtyEight "FiveThirtyEight")* analysis found that of the 293 Republican members of Congress on January 20, 2017, just 121 (41%) were left on January 20, 2025. There were many reasons for the turnover, including retirements and deaths, losing general and primary elections, seeking other offices, etc., but the extent of the change is still stark. There were 273 Republican members of Congress on January 20, 2025. Trump also changed his vice president and both houses of Congress had changed their top leadership.[[205]](#cite_note-Trump_era-207)

#### Conservatives

Main article: [Conservatism in the United States](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States")

See also: [Cultural conservatism](/wiki/Cultural_conservatism "Cultural conservatism"), [Fiscal conservatism](/wiki/Fiscal_conservatism "Fiscal conservatism"), [Movement conservatism](/wiki/Movement_conservatism "Movement conservatism"), [Neoconservatism](/wiki/Neoconservatism "Neoconservatism"), and [Social conservatism](/wiki/Social_conservatism "Social conservatism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Conservative_Gallup_8-10.svg/250px-Conservative_Gallup_8-10.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Conservative_Gallup_8-10.svg)

Percent of self-identified [conservatives](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") by state as of 2018, according to a [Gallup poll](/wiki/Gallup_poll "Gallup poll"):[[206]](#cite_note-Jones_2019-208)

45% and above

40–44%

35–39%

30–34%

25–29%

24% and under

Ronald Reagan's [presidential election in 1980](/wiki/1980_United_States_presidential_election "1980 United States presidential election") established Reagan-style American conservatism as the dominant ideological faction of the Republican Party until the election of Donald Trump in 2016.[[209]](#cite_note-Establishment-211) Trump's 2016 election split both the GOP and larger conservative movement into [Trumpist](/wiki/Factions_in_the_Republican_Party_(United_States)#Trumpists "Factions in the Republican Party (United States)") and [anti-Trump](/wiki/Never_Trump_movement "Never Trump movement") factions, with the Trumpist faction winning.[[210]](#cite_note-Johnson-McCray-Ragusa_2018-212)[[211]](#cite_note-Swartz2022-213) According to [Nate Silver](/wiki/Nate_Silver "Nate Silver"), in all three of Trump's runs for president, income had no significant correlation with support for the Republican Party, as voters across all incomes were closely divided between the two parties.[[160]](#cite_note-Nate_Silver-162)[[212]](#cite_note-culture_trumps_economic_class-214)

Demographically, the party has lost majority support from white voters with college degrees, while continuing to gain among voters without college degrees.[[213]](#cite_note-Lost_Their-215)[[159]](#cite_note-Polarization_by_education-161)[[155]](#cite_note-cambridge.org-157)[[114]](#cite_note-Cliffe_2023-116) Higher educational attainment is strongly correlated with higher income, as well as decreased support for Trump and social conservatism.[[114]](#cite_note-Cliffe_2023-116) In the 2024 presidential election, Democrat [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris") won a majority of voters with annual incomes over $100,000 (51–47%) and $200,000 (52–46%). Harris was also very competitive among White voters making over $100,000 (49–50%) and $200,000 a year (48–51%).[[214]](#cite_note-Exit_poll_results_2024-216)

A core economic belief of Reagan-style American conservatism that has been opposed by the right-wing populist faction is support for [neoliberalism](/wiki/Neoliberalism "Neoliberalism"),[[167]](#cite_note-Gerstle2022-169) including support for multilateralism and [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade") while opposing tariffs.[[24]](#cite_note-Trump's_aggressive_push-26) The right-wing populist faction has gained preeminence by appealing to White voters without college degrees who oppose globalization and free trade and instead support enacting tariffs,[[215]](#cite_note-favor_Trump_tariffs-217) particularly in the [Rust Belt](/wiki/Rust_Belt "Rust Belt") states that were crucial to Donald Trump winning the presidency twice.[[129]](#cite_note-The_World_Trump_Wants-131) Donald Trump and his base have supported enacting [mercantilist](/wiki/Mercantilism "Mercantilism") economic policies intended to bring back the economic model that dominated the world from roughly the 16th to 19th centuries.[[28]](#cite_note-Gilded_Age_vision-30)[[25]](#cite_note-Helleiner_2021-27)

Conventional conservatism has been in decline across the Western world, not just the United States.[[114]](#cite_note-Cliffe_2023-116) In the European Union's [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"), right-wing populist parties and [European conservative parties](/wiki/European_People%27s_Party "European People's Party") both received support from about a quarter of voters in the early 2020s, the highest share for right-wing populist parties since the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[216]](#cite_note-218)

Trump's first vice president [Mike Pence](/wiki/Mike_Pence "Mike Pence") has since distanced himself from Trump and did not endorse him in the 2024 presidential election.[[217]](#cite_note-219)[[218]](#cite_note-Pence-220) Trump did not run with Pence again, instead choosing JD Vance.[[219]](#cite_note-221) [Mitch McConnell](/wiki/Mitch_McConnell "Mitch McConnell"), who previously served as Senate Republican leader for 18 years (2007–2025), stepped down as leader in 2025 and will retire in 2026 due to declining health and age, as well as disagreements with Trump. McConnell was described as the last powerful member of the Republican establishment, with his retirement marking its end.[[220]](#cite_note-Carney_2025-222)[[109]](#cite_note-Price_of_Power-111)[[108]](#cite_note-Punchbowl_Old_GOP-110)

The [Roberts Court](/wiki/Roberts_Court "Roberts Court") (2005–present), three of whose members were appointed by Trump as of 2024, has been described as the most conservative Supreme Court since the [Vinson Court](/wiki/Vinson_Court "Vinson Court") (1946–1953). It represents the last of the Republican establishment, with [Chief Justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States") [John Roberts](/wiki/John_Roberts "John Roberts") the only Republican leader before Trump to have maintained office during Trump's second term.[[221]](#cite_note-Nine_Black_Robes-223)

The party still maintains long-time ideologically conservative positions on many issues.[[222]](#cite_note-Aratani-2021-224) Traditional modern conservatives combine support for free-market economic policies with [social conservatism](/wiki/Social_conservatism "Social conservatism") and a hawkish approach to foreign policy.[[223]](#cite_note-Devine-2014-225) Other parts of the conservative movement are composed of fiscal conservatives and [deficit hawks](/wiki/Deficit_hawk "Deficit hawk").[[224]](#cite_note-226)

In foreign policy, neoconservatives are a small faction of the GOP that support an [interventionist foreign policy](/wiki/Interventionism_(politics) "Interventionism (politics)") and increased military spending. They previously held significant influence in the early 2000s in planning the initial response to the [9/11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") through the [war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror").[[91]](#cite_note-Rathburn_2008-93) Since the election of Trump in 2016, neoconservatism has declined and [non-interventionism](/wiki/Non-interventionism "Non-interventionism") and [isolationism](/wiki/Isolationism "Isolationism") have grown among elected federal Republican officeholders.[[29]](#cite_note-New_Fusionism-31)[[225]](#cite_note-Rucker_2016-227)[[226]](#cite_note-Dodson-Brooks_2021-228)

Long-term shifts in conservative thinking following the elections of Trump have been described as a "new fusionism" of traditional conservative ideology and right-wing populist themes.[[29]](#cite_note-New_Fusionism-31) These have resulted in shifts towards greater support for [national conservatism](/wiki/National_conservatism "National conservatism"),[[227]](#cite_note-229) [protectionism](/wiki/Protectionism "Protectionism"),[[228]](#cite_note-230) cultural conservatism, a more [realist](/wiki/Realism_(international_relations) "Realism (international relations)") foreign policy, a conspiracist sub-culture, a repudiation of neoconservatism, reduced efforts to roll back entitlement programs, and a disdain for traditional checks and balances.[[29]](#cite_note-New_Fusionism-31)[[229]](#cite_note-dissolved-231) There are significant divisions within the party on the issues of abortion and LGBT rights.[[184]](#cite_note-Cohn2023-186)[[230]](#cite_note-Doherty-2023-232)

Conservative caucuses include the [Republican Study Committee](/wiki/Republican_Study_Committee "Republican Study Committee") and [Freedom Caucus](/wiki/Freedom_Caucus "Freedom Caucus").[[231]](#cite_note-233)[[232]](#cite_note-234)

#### White nationalism

Main articles: [Alt-right](/wiki/Alt-right "Alt-right") and [White nationalism in the United States](/wiki/White_nationalism_in_the_United_States "White nationalism in the United States")

See also: [Racial views of Donald Trump](/wiki/Racial_views_of_Donald_Trump "Racial views of Donald Trump") and [ICE recruitment during the second Trump administration](/wiki/ICE_recruitment_during_the_second_Trump_administration "ICE recruitment during the second Trump administration")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/PatriotFrontDC.jpg/250px-PatriotFrontDC.jpg)](/wiki/File:PatriotFrontDC.jpg)

Members of [Patriot Front](/wiki/Patriot_Front "Patriot Front") in 2025 during the March For Life

White nationalists compose a significant portion of the modern Republican Party.[[21]](#cite_note-:0-23) According to conservative writer [Rod Dreher](/wiki/Rod_Dreher "Rod Dreher"), supporters of white nationalism compose either a plurality or majority of [congressional staffers](/wiki/Congressional_staff "Congressional staff"), with 30 to 40 percent being supporters of [Nick Fuentes](/wiki/Nick_Fuentes "Nick Fuentes") and an additional amount supporting other forms of [white ethnonationalism](/wiki/Myanmar_conflict "Myanmar conflict").[[21]](#cite_note-:0-23) According to a 2025 [Manhattan Institute](/wiki/Manhattan_Institute_for_Policy_Research "Manhattan Institute for Policy Research") poll of current Republicans under 50, "a notable minority" self-described themselves as holding "racist" (31%) or "antisemitic" (25%) views.[[21]](#cite_note-:0-23)

White nationalist beliefs in the party are rapidly growing, most pronounced among younger Republicans, and have been projected by analysts to further rise as a proportion of party leaders in coming years, as older traditional conservatives are gradually replaced by a more radical youth population.[[21]](#cite_note-:0-23) Tucker Carlson's 2025 interview with [Nick Fuentes](/wiki/Nick_Fuentes "Nick Fuentes"), who leads a subfaction of the movement known as [Groypers](/wiki/Groypers "Groypers"), along with the [assassination of Charlie Kirk](/wiki/Assassination_of_Charlie_Kirk "Assassination of Charlie Kirk") in the same year, led to the breaking of the former [cordon sanitaire](/wiki/Cordon_sanitaire_(politics) "Cordon sanitaire (politics)") among many leading Republicans.[[233]](#cite_note-235) According to George Thomas, white nationalists now have significant influence over many historical conservative institutions, including [the Heritage Foundation](/wiki/The_Heritage_Foundation "The Heritage Foundation").[[21]](#cite_note-:0-23)

Along with the Democratic Party, explicit white nationalism or other forms of explicit appeal to a [white identity](/wiki/White_identity "White identity") were not significant force in the Republican Party between the 1970s and mid-2010s.[[21]](#cite_note-:0-23) The [alt-right](/wiki/Alt-right "Alt-right") movement rejected conservatism and was inspired by [paleoconservatism](/wiki/Paleoconservatism "Paleoconservatism"), [identitarianism](/wiki/Identitarian_movement "Identitarian movement"), the [Dark Enlightenment](/wiki/Dark_Enlightenment "Dark Enlightenment"), and the French [Nouvelle Droite](/wiki/Nouvelle_Droite "Nouvelle Droite"). It came to be associated with a wide array of illiberal and white nationalist groups, including [Andrew Anglin](/wiki/Andrew_Anglin "Andrew Anglin")'s *[Daily Stormer](/wiki/Daily_Stormer "Daily Stormer")*, Brad Griffin's *Occidental Dissent*, and Matthew Heimbach's [Traditionalist Worker Party](/wiki/Traditionalist_Worker_Party "Traditionalist Worker Party"). It attracted broader mainstream attention in 2015, particularly through coverage on [Steve Bannon](/wiki/Steve_Bannon "Steve Bannon")'s *[Breitbart News](/wiki/Breitbart_News "Breitbart News")*, due to alt-right support for [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump")'s [presidential campaign](/wiki/Donald_Trump_2016_presidential_campaign "Donald Trump 2016 presidential campaign"). Trump denounced the alt-right and other forms of white nationalism in his first term shortly after a [2017 white supremacist rally](/wiki/Unite_the_Right_rally "Unite the Right rally"). Scholars, journalists, and academics have noted a shift since the beginning of his second term, with the White House openly supporting far-right parties and warning of [white demographic decline](/wiki/White_demographic_decline "White demographic decline").[[21]](#cite_note-:0-23)

[ICE recruitment during the second Trump administration](/wiki/ICE_recruitment_during_the_second_Trump_administration "ICE recruitment during the second Trump administration") has routinely cited white nationalist groups such as [Patriot Front](/wiki/Patriot_Front "Patriot Front"). Another advertisement shared by the White House featured a song produced and popularized by white nationalist group [Pine Tree Riots](/w/index.php?title=Pine_Tree_Riots_(Band)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pine Tree Riots (Band) (page does not exist)"). The ad was captioned "[We'll Have Our Home Again](/wiki/We%27ll_Have_Our_Home_Again "We'll Have Our Home Again")" which was the name of the song, with "join.ice.gov" written below.[[234]](#cite_note-gais-236)[[235]](#cite_note-campbell-237)[[236]](#cite_note-nbcwhitenat-238)

#### Christian right

Main articles: [Christian right](/wiki/Christian_right "Christian right"), [Christian nationalism in the United States](/wiki/Christian_nationalism_in_the_United_States "Christian nationalism in the United States"), and [Social conservatism in the United States](/wiki/Social_conservatism_in_the_United_States "Social conservatism in the United States")

See also: [Bible Belt](/wiki/Bible_Belt "Bible Belt"), [United States anti-abortion movement](/wiki/United_States_anti-abortion_movement "United States anti-abortion movement"), and [2020s anti-LGBT movement in the United States](/wiki/2020s_anti-LGBT_movement_in_the_United_States "2020s anti-LGBT movement in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Speaker_Mike_Johnson_Official_Portrait.jpg/250px-Speaker_Mike_Johnson_Official_Portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Speaker_Mike_Johnson_Official_Portrait.jpg)

House Speaker [Mike Johnson](/wiki/Mike_Johnson "Mike Johnson") (2023–present)

Since the rise of the [Christian right](/wiki/Christian_right "Christian right") in the 1970s, the Republican Party has drawn significant support from [evangelicals](/wiki/Evangelicals "Evangelicals"), [Mormons](/wiki/Mormons "Mormons"),[[237]](#cite_note-239) [Christian nationalists](/wiki/Christian_nationalism_in_the_United_States "Christian nationalism in the United States"),[[238]](#cite_note-Contreras_02182026-240) and [traditionalist](/wiki/Traditionalist_Catholicism "Traditionalist Catholicism") [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_the_United_States "Catholic Church in the United States"), partly due to [opposition to abortion](/wiki/Opposition_to_abortion "Opposition to abortion") after *[Roe v. Wade](/wiki/Roe_v._Wade "Roe v. Wade").*[[239]](#cite_note-Williams-2022-241) The Christian right faction is characterized by strong support of [socially conservative](/wiki/Social_conservatism "Social conservatism") and far-right Christian nationalist policies.[[240]](#cite_note-242) A 2026 survey by the [Public Religion Research Institute](/wiki/Public_Religion_Research_Institute "Public Religion Research Institute") found that 56% of all Republicans identify as Christian nationalist "adherents" or "sympathizers".[[238]](#cite_note-Contreras_02182026-240) Christian conservatives seek to use the teachings of Christianity to influence law and public policy.[[241]](#cite_note-243) Compared to other Republicans, the socially conservative [Christian right](/wiki/Religious_right_in_the_United_States "Religious right in the United States") faction of the party is more likely to oppose [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBT rights in the United States"), [marijuana legalization](/wiki/Legalization_of_non-medical_cannabis_in_the_United_States "Legalization of non-medical cannabis in the United States"), and support [significantly restricting the legality of abortion](/wiki/Abortion_law_in_the_United_States_by_state "Abortion law in the United States by state").[[242]](#cite_note-244)

The Christian right is strongest in the [Bible Belt](/wiki/Bible_Belt "Bible Belt"), which covers most of the Southern United States.[[243]](#cite_note-245) [Mike Pence](/wiki/Mike_Pence "Mike Pence"), Donald Trump's vice president from 2017 to 2021, was a member of the Christian right.[[244]](#cite_note-246) In October 2023, a member of the Christian right faction, Louisiana representative [Mike Johnson](/wiki/Mike_Johnson "Mike Johnson"), was elected the 56th Speaker of the United States House of Representatives.[[245]](#cite_note-247)[[246]](#cite_note-248)

#### Libertarians

Main articles: [Libertarian Republican](/wiki/Libertarian_Republican "Libertarian Republican") and [Right-libertarianism](/wiki/Right-libertarianism "Right-libertarianism")

See also: [Libertarian conservatism](/wiki/Libertarian_conservatism "Libertarian conservatism"), [Libertarianism in the United States](/wiki/Libertarianism_in_the_United_States "Libertarianism in the United States"), [Republican Liberty Caucus](/wiki/Republican_Liberty_Caucus "Republican Liberty Caucus"), and [Tea Party movement](/wiki/Tea_Party_movement "Tea Party movement")

The Republican Party has a [libertarian](/wiki/Right-libertarian "Right-libertarian") faction.[[15]](#cite_note-Wilbur-2012-16)[[184]](#cite_note-Cohn2023-186) This faction of the party is most popular in the [Midwestern](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") and Western United States.[[184]](#cite_note-Cohn2023-186) Libertarianism emerged from [fusionism](/wiki/Fusionism "Fusionism") in the 1950s and 60s.[[247]](#cite_note-249) [Barry Goldwater](/wiki/Barry_Goldwater "Barry Goldwater") had a substantial impact on the conservative-libertarian movement of the 1960s.[[248]](#cite_note-250) Compared to other Republicans, they are more likely to favor the [legalization of marijuana](/wiki/Legalization_of_non-medical_cannabis_in_the_United_States "Legalization of non-medical cannabis in the United States"), [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBT rights in the United States") such as [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage"), [gun rights](/wiki/Gun_rights "Gun rights"), oppose [mass surveillance](/wiki/Surveillance "Surveillance"), and support reforms to current laws surrounding [civil asset forfeiture](/wiki/Civil_forfeiture_in_the_United_States "Civil forfeiture in the United States"). Right-wing libertarians are [strongly divided on the subject of abortion](/wiki/Libertarian_perspectives_on_abortion "Libertarian perspectives on abortion").[[249]](#cite_note-Libertarians_for_Life-251) Prominent libertarian conservatives within the Republican Party include [Rand Paul](/wiki/Rand_Paul "Rand Paul"),[[250]](#cite_note-courier-journal.com-252)[[251]](#cite_note-hannitycpac-253) [Thomas Massie](/wiki/Thomas_Massie "Thomas Massie"),[[252]](#cite_note-tea_party-254) and [Mike Lee](/wiki/Mike_Lee "Mike Lee").[[250]](#cite_note-courier-journal.com-252)[[253]](#cite_note-255)

During the [2024 United States elections](/wiki/2024_United_States_elections "2024 United States elections"), the Republican Party adopted pro-[cryptocurrency](/wiki/Cryptocurrency "Cryptocurrency") policies, which were originally advocated by the libertarian wing of the party.[[254]](#cite_note-256) As the Republican presidential nominee, [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") addressed the [2024 Libertarian National Convention](/wiki/2024_Libertarian_National_Convention "2024 Libertarian National Convention"), pledging support for cryptocurrency, opposing [central bank digital currency](/wiki/Central_bank_digital_currency "Central bank digital currency") and expressing support for the commutation of [Ross Ulbricht](/wiki/Ross_Ulbricht "Ross Ulbricht").[[255]](#cite_note-257) Trump's 2024 campaign featured greater influence from [technolibertarian](/wiki/Technolibertarian "Technolibertarian") elements, particularly [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk"), who was subsequently nominated to lead the [Department of Government Efficiency](/wiki/Department_of_Government_Efficiency "Department of Government Efficiency") (DOGE).[[256]](#cite_note-258)[[257]](#cite_note-259)[[258]](#cite_note-260)

In June 2025, the libertarian wing strongly opposed Trump's [One Big Beautiful Bill Act](/wiki/One_Big_Beautiful_Bill_Act "One Big Beautiful Bill Act") for contributing to the national debt, with Paul, Massie, and Musk all publicly criticizing the legislation.[[259]](#cite_note-261)[[260]](#cite_note-262) In response, White House Deputy Chief of Staff for Policy [Stephen Miller](/wiki/Stephen_Miller_(advisor) "Stephen Miller (advisor)"), a figure associated with the populist wing of the party, criticized the libertarian faction, stating, "Certain libertarians in Congress, who are not MAGA, have their own agenda...and it's not yours."[[153]](#cite_note-BBBmiller-155) Miller has been described by *[The Hill](/wiki/The_Hill_(newspaper) "The Hill (newspaper)")* as advocating for a break with the libertarians within the party, based on his view that they do not sufficiently emphasize [strict immigration restrictions](/wiki/Immigration_reduction "Immigration reduction") or favor massive increases of government spending.[[261]](#cite_note-263)

#### Moderates

Main articles: [Centrism](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism") and [Center-right politics](/wiki/Center-right_politics "Center-right politics")

See also: [Republican Governance Group](/wiki/Republican_Governance_Group "Republican Governance Group"), [Moderate conservatism](/wiki/Moderate_conservatism "Moderate conservatism"), and [Problem Solvers Caucus](/wiki/Problem_Solvers_Caucus "Problem Solvers Caucus")

Moderates in the Republican Party are a now small, ideologically centrist group that predominantly come from the Northeastern United States,[[262]](#cite_note-Kashinsky-2023-264) and are typically located in [swing states](/wiki/Swing_state "Swing state") or [blue states](/wiki/Red_states_and_blue_states "Red states and blue states"). Moderate Republican voters are typically highly educated, affluent, fiscally conservative, socially moderate or liberal and often "[Never Trump](/wiki/Never_Trump_movement "Never Trump movement")".[[184]](#cite_note-Cohn2023-186)[[262]](#cite_note-Kashinsky-2023-264) While they sometimes share the economic views of other Republicans (i.e. [lower taxes](/wiki/Tax_cuts "Tax cuts"), [deregulation](/wiki/Deregulation "Deregulation"), and [welfare reform](/wiki/Welfare_reform "Welfare reform")), moderate Republicans differ in that some are for [affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action_in_the_United_States "Affirmative action in the United States"),[[263]](#cite_note-265) [LGBT rights and same-sex marriage](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBT rights in the United States"), legal access to and even public funding for [abortion](/wiki/Abortion_debate "Abortion debate"), [gun control](/wiki/Gun_politics_in_the_United_States "Gun politics in the United States") laws, more [environmental regulation](/wiki/Environmental_regulation "Environmental regulation") and action on [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), fewer restrictions on immigration and a path to citizenship for illegal immigrants.[[264]](#cite_note-266) In the 21st century, some former Republican moderates have switched to the Democratic Party,[[265]](#cite_note-267)[[266]](#cite_note-268)[[267]](#cite_note-269) and the faction is in decline.[[268]](#cite_note-h183-270)[[269]](#cite_note-a610-271)[[270]](#cite_note-k148-272)[[271]](#cite_note-i458-273)[[272]](#cite_note-y651-274)[[273]](#cite_note-y851-275)

Notable moderate Republicans include Senators [Lisa Murkowski](/wiki/Lisa_Murkowski "Lisa Murkowski") of Alaska and [Susan Collins](/wiki/Susan_Collins_(politician) "Susan Collins (politician)") of Maine,[[274]](#cite_note-276)[[275]](#cite_note-277)[[276]](#cite_note-278)[[277]](#cite_note-279) Nevada governor [Joe Lombardo](/wiki/Joe_Lombardo "Joe Lombardo"), Vermont governor [Phil Scott](/wiki/Phil_Scott "Phil Scott"),[[278]](#cite_note-280) New Hampshire governor [Kelly Ayotte](/wiki/Kelly_Ayotte "Kelly Ayotte"), and former Maryland governor [Larry Hogan](/wiki/Larry_Hogan "Larry Hogan").[[279]](#cite_note-281)[[280]](#cite_note-282)

## Political views

Main article: [Political positions of the Republican Party](/wiki/Political_positions_of_the_Republican_Party "Political positions of the Republican Party")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/V-Dem_Electoral_and_Liberal_Democracy_Indices_for_the_United_States%2C_1900%E2%80%932024.svg/500px-V-Dem_Electoral_and_Liberal_Democracy_Indices_for_the_United_States%2C_1900%E2%80%932024.svg.png)](/wiki/File:V-Dem_Electoral_and_Liberal_Democracy_Indices_for_the_United_States,_1900%E2%80%932024.svg)

The [V-Dem Institute](/wiki/V-Dem_Institute "V-Dem Institute") said in 2026 that "the speed with which American democracy is currently dismantled is unprecedented in modern history".[[281]](#cite_note-VDEM_Report_2026-283) The institute noted executive overreach undermining the [rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_law "Rule of law"), suppression and intimidation of media and dissenting voices, loss of legislative constraints, and declining [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights "Civil rights"), equality, and [freedom of expression](/wiki/Freedom_of_expression "Freedom of expression") under the Republican Party.[[281]](#cite_note-VDEM_Report_2026-283)

The Republican Party was generally regarded by political scientists as a typical Western [center-right](/wiki/Center-right "Center-right") or [right-wing](/wiki/Right-wing_politics "Right-wing politics"), [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism "Conservatism") party for a majority of its existence.[[282]](#cite_note-284)[[283]](#cite_note-:02-285) Beginning in 2017, and particularly after 2025, political scientists have described the party as [far-right](/wiki/Far-right_politics "Far-right politics") and [authoritarian](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism") at a national level.[[283]](#cite_note-:02-285) In March 2026, this view was affirmed by the [V-DEM Institute](/wiki/V-Dem_Institute "V-Dem Institute"),[[284]](#cite_note-:1-286) which is the "the gold standard for academic-quality metrics" on regime types and political parties.[[285]](#cite_note-:2-287)[[286]](#cite_note-:3-288) The report stated that the organization is "not alone in this assessment" and that it represented the general view of the political science field.[[284]](#cite_note-:1-286)

Political scientists [Pippa Norris](/wiki/Pippa_Norris "Pippa Norris"), Stuart J. Dugarte-Turnball, and José Rama have argued that "Trump's transformation of the GOP into an authoritarian populist party makes it a prime example of the wider family of far-right parties".[[287]](#cite_note-289) In 2022, Dutch political scientist [Cas Muddle](/wiki/Cas_Mudde "Cas Mudde") wrote that the party was analogous to far-right European populist parties.[[288]](#cite_note-290) In 2025, Kofi Arhin reported that the "whole party" had been "shifted" "towards the far-right".[[289]](#cite_note-291) In 2026, Bright Line Watch found a "near-unanimous" consensus among political scientists that Trump and the Republican Party are dismantling democracy in the United States. The findings were similar to other metrics of political regimes and party ideologies conducted since Trump's second term.[[290]](#cite_note-292) In early 2026, the [V-DEM Institute](/wiki/V-Dem_Institute "V-Dem Institute") reported that Trump and the Republican Party are "aiming for [dictatorship](/wiki/Dictatorship "Dictatorship")".[[285]](#cite_note-:2-287)[[286]](#cite_note-:3-288) Its head, political scientist [Staffan I. Lindberg](/wiki/Staffan_I._Lindberg "Staffan I. Lindberg"), personally claimed that both were acting in ways that directly contradict [the values](/wiki/Americanism_(ideology) "Americanism (ideology)") of the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") and the [Founding Fathers of the United States](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States"), supporting an [executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") with [near-unlimited power](/wiki/Autocracy "Autocracy").[[285]](#cite_note-:2-287)

The election of Trump in 2016 saw the Republican Party shift to embrace and bring [far-right](/wiki/Far-right "Far-right"), fringe, and extreme ideas and organizations into the mainstream.[[291]](#cite_note-293)[[292]](#cite_note-294)[[293]](#cite_note-295)[[294]](#cite_note-296) His election shifted traditional Republican beliefs and ideology into a new leadership style and political agenda referred to as Trumpism.[[9]](#cite_note-Ball_2024-9)[[10]](#cite_note-v075-10)[[11]](#cite_note-i277-11)

Trump explicitly and routinely disparages racial, religious, and ethnic minorities,[[295]](#cite_note-297) and scholars consistently find that racial animus regarding blacks, immigrants, and Muslims are the best predictors of support for Trump.[[166]](#cite_note-Berman-168) By 2025, *The New York Times* reported that congressional Republicans had increasingly used overtly bigoted language and offensive tropes against the racial and religious identity of their political opponents with little to no pushback from GOP leadership.[[296]](#cite_note-298)

The second presidency of Trump saw him nominate several White House officials with ties to [antisemitic](/wiki/Antisemitic "Antisemitic") extremists.[[297]](#cite_note-299) His administration promoted social media content promoting [remigration](/wiki/Remigration "Remigration") and containing antisemitic slurs,[[298]](#cite_note-300) and was criticized as promoting [white nationalism](/wiki/White_nationalism "White nationalism") and [Nazism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism").[[299]](#cite_note-SPLC_2025-301)[[300]](#cite_note-302) The [Southern Poverty Law Center](/wiki/Southern_Poverty_Law_Center "Southern Poverty Law Center") found that some "images and language appear to come directly from antisemitic and neo-Nazi publications and a white Christian nationalist website".[[299]](#cite_note-SPLC_2025-301)

In October 2025, *Politico* [revealed leaked group chats](/wiki/Young_Republican_group_chat_leaks "Young Republican group chat leaks") among high-ranking leaders of the "[Young Republicans](/wiki/Young_Republicans "Young Republicans")" that included language praising [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") and promoting Nazism, encouraging the rape and killing of political opponents, extensive use of antisemitic and racial slurs, and favorable opinions on [slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery").[[301]](#cite_note-303) The messages drew bipartisan condemnation, and [another incident](/wiki/David_Taylor_swastika_incident "David Taylor swastika incident") involving the display of an American flag with a [swastika](/wiki/Swastika "Swastika") in Republican Congressman [David Taylor](/wiki/David_Taylor_(Ohio_politician) "David Taylor (Ohio politician)")'s office a day later spurred significant political commentary about the future of the GOP and condemnation about the prominence of Nazi ideology within the Republican Party.[[302]](#cite_note-304)[[303]](#cite_note-305)[[304]](#cite_note-306)[[305]](#cite_note-307)[[306]](#cite_note-308) The same month, *Politico* posted more leaked messages from Republican Office of Special Council nominee [Paul Ingrassia](/wiki/Paul_Ingrassia_(lawyer) "Paul Ingrassia (lawyer)") where he stated he had "a Nazi streak", that he wanted white men in positions of leadership, used ethnic slurs for Black people and Italians, and proposed making [Kwanzaa](/wiki/Kwanzaa "Kwanzaa") and all celebrations of Black culture illegal.[[307]](#cite_note-309)

Also starting in October 2025, *[Axios](/wiki/Axios_(website) "Axios (website)")*, *Politico*, *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")*, *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")*, and *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* described the party as undergoing a "rift" among a growing cohort of anti-Jewish and antisemitic voices within the party and debate over how expansive the party's coalition should be.[[308]](#cite_note-310)[[309]](#cite_note-311)[[22]](#cite_note-Economist_12232025-24)[[310]](#cite_note-312)[[311]](#cite_note-313) In January 2026, Trump publicly came out against those with antisemitic views in the party, which *The New York Times* and *The Economist* described as being contrasted with J.D. Vance's prior refusal to condemn the incidents and statement that the party did not need "purity tests".[[312]](#cite_note-314)[[22]](#cite_note-Economist_12232025-24)

### Economic policies

[Enacting high tariffs](/wiki/Tariffs_in_the_second_Trump_administration "Tariffs in the second Trump administration"), taxes on foreign imports, is a core component of [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump")'s fiscal agenda. Tariffs are taxes on foreign imports, mainly paid by domestic businesses, given that consumers generally do not import foreign goods directly.[[313]](#cite_note-Buckle_Up-315) By raising tariffs to their highest levels since the [Gilded Age](/wiki/Gilded_Age "Gilded Age"), Trump enacted one of the largest tax increases by any Republican president.[[314]](#cite_note-Trump_Is_Raising_Them-316) The Constitution's [Import-Export Clause](/wiki/Import-Export_Clause "Import-Export Clause") requires that only the federal government be allowed to collect tariff revenue from imports.[[28]](#cite_note-Gilded_Age_vision-30)

Traditionally, Republicans believe that [free markets](/wiki/Free_market "Free market") and individual achievement are the primary factors behind economic prosperity.[[315]](#cite_note-317) Reduction in income taxes for those with higher incomes[[316]](#cite_note-318)[[317]](#cite_note-319) is a core component of Republicans' fiscal agenda.[[318]](#cite_note-320) As of 2025, Trump and the Republican Party largely abandoned traditional Republican orthodoxy about protecting and promoting the free market,[[319]](#cite_note-321)[[320]](#cite_note-Morrow_08202025-322)[[321]](#cite_note-Wiles_08222025-323)[[322]](#cite_note-324) instead favoring [state capitalism](/wiki/State_capitalism "State capitalism") by taking direct government equity stakes in major US corporations, an approach that has been characterized by critics in favor of free market economics as [socialist](/wiki/Socialist "Socialist").[[323]](#cite_note-325)[[324]](#cite_note-326)[[325]](#cite_note-327)[[326]](#cite_note-Economist_08132025-328)[[327]](#cite_note-329)[[328]](#cite_note-330) The Trump administration has also deviated from the GOP's conventional free market approach by ordering [Nvidia](/wiki/Nvidia "Nvidia") and [AMD](/wiki/AMD "AMD") to pay the federal government a portion of chip sales to China.[[329]](#cite_note-331)

#### Taxes and trade

As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Republican_Party_(United_States)&action=edit) the Republican Party supports near-universal [tariffs](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff"), but that has not always been the case. For example, during the last half of the 20th century, Republicans were strong proponents of [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade"). However 68% of Republicans and Republican-leaning independents approve of the increasing tariffs while the Democrats overwhelmingly disapprove of it at 89%.[[330]](#cite_note-332) The current Republican president, Donald Trump, has been a staunch proponent of enacting [tariffs](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff") as a means of generating tax revenue and has been described as a [mercantilist](/wiki/Mercantilism "Mercantilism").[[25]](#cite_note-Helleiner_2021-27)[[331]](#cite_note-333) In 2025, Trump raised American tariff rates to the highest in the world, at the highest level since the [Smoot–Hawley Tariff Act](/wiki/Smoot%E2%80%93Hawley_Tariff_Act "Smoot–Hawley Tariff Act") of 1930.[[332]](#cite_note-global_trade_war-334) Donald Trump opposes [globalization](/wiki/Economic_globalization "Economic globalization"), and his economic policies have been described as attempting to unravel the multilateral global economic order, including the power of the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") (WTO).[[24]](#cite_note-Trump's_aggressive_push-26) Trump has expressed his admiration for Republican president [William McKinley](/wiki/William_McKinley "William McKinley")'s tariff policies. McKinley was the author of the [Tariff Act of 1890](/wiki/Tariff_Act_of_1890 "Tariff Act of 1890"), and both Trump and McKinley nicknamed themselves as a "Tariff Man".[[333]](#cite_note-President_McKinley-335)[[313]](#cite_note-Buckle_Up-315) According to an April 2025 Economist/[YouGov](/wiki/YouGov "YouGov") poll, "Republican voters overwhelmingly support Trump's tariffs, while Democratic voters generally do not."[[215]](#cite_note-favor_Trump_tariffs-217)

At its inception, the Republican Party supported [protective tariffs](/wiki/Tariffs_in_United_States_history "Tariffs in United States history"). Abraham Lincoln enacted tariffs during the Civil War.[[334]](#cite_note-Coy_F._Cross_II_2012_45-336)[[335]](#cite_note-Platform-337) The great battle over the high [Payne–Aldrich Tariff Act](/wiki/Payne%E2%80%93Aldrich_Tariff_Act "Payne–Aldrich Tariff Act") in 1910 caused a split in the party.[[336]](#cite_note-338) The [Reciprocal Tariff Act](/wiki/Reciprocal_Tariff_Act "Reciprocal Tariff Act") of 1934 marked a sharp departure from the era of [protectionism](/wiki/Protectionism "Protectionism") in the United States. American duties on foreign products declined from an average of 46% in 1934 to 12% by 1962, which included the presidency of Republican president [Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower").[[337]](#cite_note-Bailey-339) After World War II, the U.S. promoted the [General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade](/wiki/General_Agreement_on_Tariffs_and_Trade "General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade") (GATT) established in 1947, to minimize tariffs and other restrictions, and to liberalize trade among all capitalist countries.[[338]](#cite_note-barton-340)[[339]](#cite_note-341)
During the [Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") and [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") administrations, Republicans abandoned protectionist policies[[340]](#cite_note-Karagiannis-342) and came out against quotas and in favor of the GATT and the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") policy of minimal economic barriers to global trade. Free trade with Canada came about as a result of the [Canada–United States Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_Free_Trade_Agreement "Canada–United States Free Trade Agreement") of 1987, which led in 1994 to the [North American Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/North_American_Free_Trade_Agreement "North American Free Trade Agreement") (NAFTA) based on Reagan's plan to enlarge the scope of the market for American firms to include Canada and Mexico. President [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), with strong Republican support in 1993, pushed NAFTA through Congress over the vehement objection of labor unions.[[341]](#cite_note-343)[[342]](#cite_note-344)

The 2016 presidential election marked a return to supporting protectionism, beginning with [Donald Trump's first presidency](/wiki/First_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "First presidency of Donald Trump").[[343]](#cite_note-345)[[344]](#cite_note-Swanson-346) In 2017, only 36% of Republicans agreed that free trade agreements are good for the United States, compared to 67% of Democrats. When asked if free trade has helped respondents specifically, the approval numbers for Democrats drop to 54%, however approval ratings among Republicans remain relatively unchanged at 34%.[[345]](#cite_note-347)

Income tax cuts have been at the core of Republican economic policy since 1980.[[346]](#cite_note-348) At the national level and state level, Republicans tend to pursue policies of tax cuts and deregulation.[[347]](#cite_note-Grumbach-2021-349) Modern Republicans advocate the theory of [supply-side economics](/wiki/Supply_side_economics "Supply side economics"), which holds that lower tax rates increase economic growth.[[348]](#cite_note-350) Many Republicans oppose [higher tax rates for higher earners](/wiki/Progressive_taxation "Progressive taxation"), which they believe are unfairly targeted at those who create jobs and wealth. They believe private spending is more efficient than government spending. Republican lawmakers have also sought to limit funding for tax enforcement and [tax collection](/wiki/Revenue_service "Revenue service").[[349]](#cite_note-How_the_IRS_Was_Gutted-351)

The modern Republican Party's economic policy positions tend to align with business interests and the affluent.[[350]](#cite_note-352)[[351]](#cite_note-353)[[352]](#cite_note-354)[[353]](#cite_note-355)[[354]](#cite_note-356)

Republicans have traditionally advocated in favor of [fiscal conservatism](/wiki/Fiscal_conservatism "Fiscal conservatism").[[355]](#cite_note-357)[[356]](#cite_note-358)[[357]](#cite_note-359) By the 2020s, Republicans have largely abandoned fiscal conservatism as an ideological cornerstone.[[358]](#cite_note-360)

#### Labor unions and the minimum wage

The Republican Party is generally opposed to labor unions.[[359]](#cite_note-361)[[360]](#cite_note-362) Republicans believe corporations should be able to establish their own employment practices, including benefits and wages, with the free market deciding the price of work. Since the 1920s, Republicans have generally been opposed by [labor union](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") organizations and members. At the national level, Republicans supported the [Taft–Hartley Act](/wiki/Taft%E2%80%93Hartley_Act "Taft–Hartley Act") of 1947, which gives workers the right not to participate in unions. Modern Republicans at the state level generally support various [right-to-work laws](/wiki/Right-to-work_laws "Right-to-work laws").[[c]](#cite_note-364) Most Republicans also oppose increases in the [minimum wage](/wiki/Minimum_wage "Minimum wage").[[362]](#cite_note-365)

#### Environmental policies

Main article: [Political positions of the Republican Party § Environmental policies](/wiki/Political_positions_of_the_Republican_Party#Environmental_policies "Political positions of the Republican Party")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/2009-_Pew_survey_-_is_climate_change_a_major_threat%2C_by_political_party.svg/250px-2009-_Pew_survey_-_is_climate_change_a_major_threat%2C_by_political_party.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2009-_Pew_survey_-_is_climate_change_a_major_threat,_by_political_party.svg)

Democrats and Republicans have diverged on the seriousness of the threat posed by climate change, with Republicans' assessment remaining essentially unchanged over the past decade.[[363]](#cite_note-PewClimateChange_20230418-366)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/20220301_Opinions_by_political_party_-_Climate_change_causation_-_Action_for_carbon_neutral_2050_-_Pew_Research.svg/250px-20220301_Opinions_by_political_party_-_Climate_change_causation_-_Action_for_carbon_neutral_2050_-_Pew_Research.svg.png)](/wiki/File:20220301_Opinions_by_political_party_-_Climate_change_causation_-_Action_for_carbon_neutral_2050_-_Pew_Research.svg)

Opinion about human causation of climate change increased substantially with education among Democrats, but not among Republicans.[[364]](#cite_note-Pew_20220301-367) Conversely, opinions favoring becoming carbon neutral declined substantially with age among Republicans, but not among Democrats.[[364]](#cite_note-Pew_20220301-367)

Historically, [progressive](/wiki/Progressivism_in_the_United_States "Progressivism in the United States") leaders in the Republican Party supported [environmental protection](/wiki/Environmental_protection "Environmental protection"). Republican President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") was a prominent [conservationist](/wiki/Conservation_(ethic) "Conservation (ethic)") whose policies eventually led to the creation of the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service").[[365]](#cite_note-Filler-368) While Republican President [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") was not an environmentalist, he signed legislation to create the [Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency") in 1970 and had a comprehensive environmental program.[[366]](#cite_note-369) However, this position has changed since the 1980s and the administration of President [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan"), who labeled environmental regulations a burden on the economy.[[367]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2010-370) Since then, Republicans have increasingly taken positions against environmental regulation,[[368]](#cite_note-371)[[369]](#cite_note-372)[[370]](#cite_note-373) with many Republicans rejecting the [scientific consensus on climate change](/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change "Scientific consensus on climate change").[[367]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2010-370)[[371]](#cite_note-374)[[372]](#cite_note-Ringquist-375)[[373]](#cite_note-Shipan_Environmental_Policy-376) Republican voters are divided over the human causes of climate change and global warming.[[374]](#cite_note-377) Since 2008,[[375]](#cite_note-Davenport2017-378) many members of the Republican Party have been criticized for being [anti-environmentalist](/wiki/Anti-environmentalism "Anti-environmentalism")[[376]](#cite_note-379)[[377]](#cite_note-380)[[378]](#cite_note-381) and promoting [climate change denial](/wiki/Climate_change_denial "Climate change denial")[[367]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2010-370)[[379]](#cite_note-382)[[380]](#cite_note-chait-383) in opposition to the general [scientific consensus](/wiki/Scientific_opinion_on_climate_change "Scientific opinion on climate change"), making them unique even among other worldwide conservative parties.[[380]](#cite_note-chait-383)

In 2006, then-[California Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_California "Governor of California") [Arnold Schwarzenegger](/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger") broke from Republican orthodoxy to sign several bills imposing caps on [carbon emissions](/wiki/Carbon_emissions "Carbon emissions") in California. Then-President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") opposed mandatory caps at a national level. Bush's decision not to regulate carbon dioxide as a pollutant was [challenged in the Supreme Court by 12 states](/wiki/Massachusetts_v._Environmental_Protection_Agency "Massachusetts v. Environmental Protection Agency"),[[381]](#cite_note-Landmark_Law-384) with the court ruling against the Bush administration in 2007.[[382]](#cite_note-385) Bush also publicly opposed ratification of the [Kyoto Protocols](/wiki/Kyoto_Protocol "Kyoto Protocol")[[367]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2010-370)[[383]](#cite_note-BushGW-386) which sought to limit greenhouse gas emissions and thereby [combat climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation"); his position was heavily criticized by climate scientists.[[384]](#cite_note-387)

The Republican Party rejects [cap-and-trade](/wiki/Emissions_trading "Emissions trading") policy to limit carbon emissions.[[385]](#cite_note-388) In the 2000s, Senator [John McCain](/wiki/John_McCain "John McCain") proposed bills (such as the [McCain-Lieberman Climate Stewardship Act](/wiki/McCain-Lieberman_Climate_Stewardship_Act "McCain-Lieberman Climate Stewardship Act")) that would have regulated carbon emissions, but his position on climate change was unusual among high-ranking party members.[[367]](#cite_note-Dunlap_2010-370) Some Republican candidates have supported the development of [alternative fuels](/wiki/Alternative_fuel "Alternative fuel") to achieve [energy independence for the United States](/wiki/U.S._energy_independence "U.S. energy independence"). Some Republicans support increased [oil drilling](/wiki/Oil_well "Oil well") in protected areas such as the [Arctic National Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Arctic_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Arctic National Wildlife Refuge"), a position that has drawn criticism from activists.[[386]](#cite_note-389)

Many Republicans during the [presidency of Barack Obama](/wiki/Presidency_of_Barack_Obama "Presidency of Barack Obama") opposed his administration's new environmental regulations, such as those on carbon emissions from coal. In particular, many Republicans supported building the [Keystone Pipeline](/wiki/Keystone_Pipeline "Keystone Pipeline"); this position was supported by businesses, but opposed by indigenous peoples' groups and environmental activists.[[387]](#cite_note-390)[[388]](#cite_note-391)[[389]](#cite_note-392)

According to the [Center for American Progress](/wiki/Center_for_American_Progress "Center for American Progress"), a non-profit liberal advocacy group, more than 55% of congressional Republicans were [climate change deniers](/wiki/Climate_change_denial "Climate change denial") in 2014.[[390]](#cite_note-msnbc20140512-393)[[391]](#cite_note-charlestongazette20141222-394) [PolitiFact](/wiki/PolitiFact "PolitiFact") in May 2014 found "relatively few Republican members of Congress ... accept the prevailing scientific conclusion that [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming") is both real and man-made." The group found eight members who acknowledged it, although the group acknowledged there could be more and that not all members of Congress have taken a stance on the issue.[[392]](#cite_note-395)[[393]](#cite_note-396)

From 2008 to 2017, the Republican Party went from "debating how to combat human-caused climate change to arguing that it does not exist", according to *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*.[[375]](#cite_note-Davenport2017-378) In January 2015, the Republican-led U.S. Senate voted 98–1 to pass a resolution acknowledging that "climate change is real and is not a hoax"; however, an amendment stating that "human activity significantly contributes to climate change" was supported by only five Republican senators.[[394]](#cite_note-397) By 2025, the GOP during the second Trump administration moved to abandon all efforts to regulate greenhouse gases by reversing the [endangerment finding](/wiki/Endangerment_finding "Endangerment finding"), and Trump criticized global warming as a "hoax" and a "con job".[[395]](#cite_note-398) The Energy Department also released [a report](/wiki/A_Critical_Review_of_Impacts_of_Greenhouse_Gas_Emissions_on_the_U.S._Climate "A Critical Review of Impacts of Greenhouse Gas Emissions on the U.S. Climate") promoting climate change denial and refuting the scientific consensus on climate change after several climate skeptics were given government jobs.[[396]](#cite_note-399)

#### Health care

The party opposes a [single-payer health care](/wiki/Single-payer_health_care "Single-payer health care") system,[[397]](#cite_note-400)[[398]](#cite_note-401) describing it as [socialized medicine](/wiki/Socialized_medicine "Socialized medicine"). It also opposes the [Affordable Care Act](/wiki/Affordable_Care_Act "Affordable Care Act")[[399]](#cite_note-402) and expansions of Medicaid.[[400]](#cite_note-403) Historically, there have been diverse and overlapping views within both the Republican Party and the Democratic Party on the role of government in health care, but the two parties became highly polarized on the topic during 2008–2009 and onwards.[[401]](#cite_note-Hacker-2010-404)

Both Republicans and [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") made various proposals to establish federally funded aged health insurance prior to the bipartisan effort to establish [Medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(United_States) "Medicare (United States)") and [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid") in 1965.[[402]](#cite_note-405)[[403]](#cite_note-406)[[404]](#cite_note-Zeitz-2017-407) No Republican member of Congress voted for the [Affordable Care Act](/wiki/Affordable_Care_Act "Affordable Care Act") in 2009, and after it passed, the party made frequent attempts to repeal it.[[401]](#cite_note-Hacker-2010-404)[[405]](#cite_note-408) At the state level, the party has tended to adopt a position against [Medicaid expansion](/wiki/Medicaid_expansion "Medicaid expansion").[[347]](#cite_note-Grumbach-2021-349)[[404]](#cite_note-Zeitz-2017-407)

Republicans typically believe individuals should take responsibility for their own circumstances and that the private sector is more effective in helping the poor through charity than the government is through welfare programs, and argue that social assistance programs cause government dependency.[[406]](#cite_note-409) As of November 2022, all 11 states that had not expanded Medicaid had Republican-controlled [state legislatures](/wiki/State_legislature_(United_States) "State legislature (United States)").[[407]](#cite_note-KaiserMedicaid-410)

By 2020, Republican officials had increasingly adopted [anti-vaccine activism](/wiki/Anti-vaccine_activism "Anti-vaccine activism") and policy.[[408]](#cite_note-411)

### Foreign policy

See also: [History of foreign policy and national defense in the Republican Party](/wiki/History_of_foreign_policy_and_national_defense_in_the_Republican_Party "History of foreign policy and national defense in the Republican Party")
