The Republican Party has a persistent history of skepticism and opposition to multilateralism in American foreign policy.[[409]](#cite_note-412) [Neoconservatism](/wiki/Neoconservatism "Neoconservatism"), which supports [unilateralism](/wiki/Unilateralism "Unilateralism") and emphasizes the use of force and hawkishness in American foreign policy, has had some influence in all Republican presidential administrations since Ronald Reagan's.[[410]](#cite_note-413) Some, including [paleoconservatives](/wiki/Paleoconservatism "Paleoconservatism"),[[411]](#cite_note-414) call for [non-interventionism](/wiki/Non-interventionism "Non-interventionism") and an [isolationist](/wiki/Isolationism "Isolationism") "[America First](/wiki/America_First "America First")" foreign policy agenda.[[29]](#cite_note-New_Fusionism-31)[[225]](#cite_note-Rucker_2016-227)[[226]](#cite_note-Dodson-Brooks_2021-228) This faction gained strength starting in 2016 with the rise of Donald Trump, demanding that the United States reset its previous [interventionist](/wiki/Interventionism_(politics) "Interventionism (politics)") foreign policy and encourage allies and partners to take greater responsibility for their own defense.[[412]](#cite_note-415)

#### Israel

During the 1940s, many Republicans, particularly Senator [Robert A. Taft](/wiki/Robert_A._Taft "Robert A. Taft"), advocated for recognition of Israel,[[413]](#cite_note-416) leading to support for Israel being integrated into the 1948 Republican Party platform.[[414]](#cite_note-417) Nevertheless, some Republicans at the time opposed the cause of an independent Jewish state due to the influence of [conservatives](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") of the [Old Right](/wiki/Old_Right_(United_States) "Old Right (United States)").[[415]](#cite_note-Cavari-2020-418) The rise of [neoconservatism](/wiki/Neoconservatism "Neoconservatism") saw the Republican Party become further pro-Israel by the 1990s and 2000s,[[416]](#cite_note-419) although notable anti-Israel sentiment persisted through [paleoconservative](/wiki/Paleoconservatism "Paleoconservatism") figures such as [Pat Buchanan](/wiki/Pat_Buchanan "Pat Buchanan").[[417]](#cite_note-420) As president, Donald Trump generally supported Israel during most of his term, but became increasingly critical of Israeli Prime Minister [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu") towards the end of it.[[418]](#cite_note-421) According to *[i24NEWS](/wiki/I24NEWS_(Israeli_TV_channel) "I24NEWS (Israeli TV channel)")*, the 2020s have seen declining support for Israel among nationalist Republicans, led by individuals such as [Tucker Carlson](/wiki/Tucker_Carlson "Tucker Carlson").[[415]](#cite_note-Cavari-2020-418)[[419]](#cite_note-422) Nevertheless, the 2024 Republican Party platform reaffirmed the party would "stand with Israel" and called for the deportation of "pro-[Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas") radicals", while expressing a desire for peace in the Middle East.[[420]](#cite_note-423) Although the Republican Party has often positioned itself as an opponent of antisemitism and denounced Democrats as insufficiently supportive of Israel,[[421]](#cite_note-424) many members of the [Christian right](/wiki/Christian_right "Christian right") support Israel primarily due to [theological beliefs](/wiki/Christian_Zionism "Christian Zionism") about the centrality of Israel to the [Second Coming of Jesus Christ](/wiki/Second_Coming "Second Coming") and the conversion or [damnation](/wiki/Hell_in_Christianity "Hell in Christianity") of Jews and other non-Christians.[[422]](#cite_note-425)[[423]](#cite_note-426)

#### Taiwan

In the party's 2016 platform,[[424]](#cite_note-amazonaws1-427) its stance on [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") is: "We oppose any unilateral steps by either side to alter the status quo in the Taiwan Straits on the principle that all issues regarding the island's future must be resolved peacefully, through dialogue, and be agreeable to the people of Taiwan." In addition, if "China were to violate those principles, the United States, in accord with the [Taiwan Relations Act](/wiki/Taiwan_Relations_Act "Taiwan Relations Act"), will help Taiwan defend itself".

Mention of Taiwan was omitted from the party's 2024 platform.[[425]](#cite_note-428)

#### War on terror

Main article: [War on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror")

Further information: [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks")

Since the terrorist attacks on September 11, 2001, neoconservatives in the party have supported the war on terror, including the [War in Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)") and the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"). The [George W. Bush administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_George_W._Bush "Presidency of George W. Bush") took the position that the [Geneva Conventions](/wiki/Geneva_Conventions "Geneva Conventions") do not apply to [unlawful combatants](/wiki/Unlawful_combatant "Unlawful combatant"), while other prominent Republicans, such as [Ted Cruz](/wiki/Ted_Cruz "Ted Cruz"), strongly oppose the use of [enhanced interrogation techniques](/wiki/Enhanced_interrogation_techniques "Enhanced interrogation techniques"), which they view as torture.[[426]](#cite_note-429) In the 2020s, Trumpist Republicans such as [Matt Gaetz](/wiki/Matt_Gaetz "Matt Gaetz") supported reducing U.S. military presence abroad and ending [intervention in countries such as Somalia](/wiki/American_military_intervention_in_Somalia_(2007%E2%80%93present) "American military intervention in Somalia (2007–present)").[[427]](#cite_note-430)

#### Europe, Russia and Ukraine

See also: [United States and the Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/United_States_and_the_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "United States and the Russian invasion of Ukraine")

The 2016 Republican platform eliminated references to giving weapons to [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") in its fight with Russia and [rebel forces](/wiki/Separatist_forces_of_the_war_in_Donbass "Separatist forces of the war in Donbass"); the removal of this language reportedly resulted from intervention from staffers to presumptive Republican presidential nominee Donald Trump.[[428]](#cite_note-431) However, the Trump administration approved a new sale of anti-tank weapons to Ukraine in 2017.[[429]](#cite_note-432) Republicans generally question European [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") members' alleged insufficient investment in defense funding, and some are dissatisfied with U.S. aid to Ukraine.[[430]](#cite_note-433)[[431]](#cite_note-434) Some Republican members of the U.S. Congress support foreign aid to Israel but not to Ukraine,[[175]](#cite_note-Falk_2023_t804-177)[[35]](#cite_note-Riccardi-37) and have been described by U.S. media as [pro-Russian](/wiki/Pro-Russian "Pro-Russian").[[184]](#cite_note-Cohn2023-186)[[177]](#cite_note-Lillis-179)[[178]](#cite_note-Ball-180)[[179]](#cite_note-Jonathan-181)[[181]](#cite_note-Lange-183)[[182]](#cite_note-New_York_Times-184)[[183]](#cite_note-Baker-185)

Amid the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine"), several prominent Republicans criticized some colleagues and conservative media outlets for echoing Russian propaganda. [Liz Cheney](/wiki/Liz_Cheney "Liz Cheney"), formerly the third-ranking House Republican, said "a [Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") wing of the Republican Party" had emerged. Former vice president [Mike Pence](/wiki/Mike_Pence "Mike Pence") said, "There is no room in the Republican Party for apologists for Putin." [House Foreign Affairs Committee](/wiki/House_Foreign_Affairs_Committee "House Foreign Affairs Committee") chairman [Michael McCaul](/wiki/Michael_McCaul "Michael McCaul") asserted that Russian propaganda had "infected a good chunk of my party's base." [House Intelligence Committee](/wiki/House_Intelligence_Committee "House Intelligence Committee") chairman [Mike Turner](/wiki/Mike_Turner "Mike Turner") confirmed McCaul's assessment, asserting that some propaganda coming directly from Russia could be heard on the House floor. Republican senator [Thom Tillis](/wiki/Thom_Tillis "Thom Tillis") characterized the influential conservative commentator [Tucker Carlson](/wiki/Tucker_Carlson "Tucker Carlson"), who frequently expresses pro-Russia sentiments, as Russia's "[useful idiot](/wiki/Useful_idiot "Useful idiot")".[[432]](#cite_note-435)[[433]](#cite_note-436)[[434]](#cite_note-437)[[435]](#cite_note-438)

In April 2024, a majority of Republican members of the [U.S. House of Representatives](/wiki/U.S._House_of_Representatives "U.S. House of Representatives") voted against a military aid package to Ukraine.[[436]](#cite_note-439) Both Trump and Senator [JD Vance](/wiki/JD_Vance "JD Vance"), the 2024 Republican presidential nominee and vice presidential nominee respectively, have been vocal critics of military aid to Ukraine and advocates of a peace deal between Russia and Ukraine.[[437]](#cite_note-440)[[438]](#cite_note-441)[[439]](#cite_note-442)[[440]](#cite_note-443) The 2024 Republican Party platform did not mention Russia or Ukraine, but stated the party's objectives to "prevent World War III" and "restore peace to Europe".[[441]](#cite_note-444)

In February 2025, during the [Trump–Zelenskyy meeting](/wiki/2025_Trump%E2%80%93Zelenskyy_Oval_Office_meeting "2025 Trump–Zelenskyy Oval Office meeting"), Trump and Vance hostilely berated Ukrainian president [Volodymyr Zelenskyy](/wiki/Volodymyr_Zelenskyy "Volodymyr Zelenskyy").[[442]](#cite_note-445)

#### Foreign relations and aid

In a 2014 poll, 59% of Republicans favored doing less abroad and focusing on the country's own problems instead.[[443]](#cite_note-446)

Republicans have frequently advocated for restricting [foreign aid](/wiki/United_States_foreign_aid "United States foreign aid") as a means of asserting the national security and immigration interests of the United States.[[444]](#cite_note-447)[[445]](#cite_note-448)[[446]](#cite_note-449)

A survey by the [Chicago Council on Global Affairs](/wiki/Chicago_Council_on_Global_Affairs "Chicago Council on Global Affairs") shows that "Trump Republicans seem to prefer a US role that is more independent, less cooperative, and more inclined to use military force to deal with the threats they see as the most pressing".[[447]](#cite_note-450)

### Social issues

The Republican Party is generally associated with [social conservative](/wiki/Social_conservative "Social conservative") policies, although it does have dissenting centrist and [libertarian](/wiki/Libertarianism_in_the_United_States "Libertarianism in the United States") factions. The social conservatives support laws that uphold their [traditional values](/wiki/Traditional_values "Traditional values"), such as [opposition to same-sex marriage](/wiki/Opposition_to_same-sex_marriage_in_the_United_States "Opposition to same-sex marriage in the United States"), abortion, and marijuana.[[448]](#cite_note-Zelizer_2004_704–5-451) The Republican Party's positions on social and cultural issues are in part a reflection of the influential role that the [Christian right](/wiki/Christian_right "Christian right") has had in the party since the 1970s.[[449]](#cite_note-452)[[450]](#cite_note-453)[[451]](#cite_note-454) Most conservative Republicans also oppose [gun control](/wiki/Gun_control "Gun control"), [affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action "Affirmative action"), and [illegal immigration](/wiki/Illegal_immigration "Illegal immigration").[[448]](#cite_note-Zelizer_2004_704–5-451)[[452]](#cite_note-455)

#### Abortion and embryonic stem cell research

The Republican position on [abortion](/wiki/Abortion_in_the_United_States "Abortion in the United States") has changed significantly over time.[[239]](#cite_note-Williams-2022-241)[[453]](#cite_note-Williams-2015-456) During the 1960s and early 1970s, opposition to abortion was concentrated among members of the political left and the Democratic Party; most liberal Catholics—which tended to vote for the Democratic Party—opposed expanding abortion access while most conservative evangelical Protestants supported it.[[453]](#cite_note-Williams-2015-456)

During this period, Republicans generally favored legalized abortion more than Democrats,[[454]](#cite_note-Halpern-2018-457)[[455]](#cite_note-458) although significant heterogeneity could be found within both parties.[[456]](#cite_note-Taylor-2018-459) Leading Republican political figures, including [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon"), [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford"), [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan"), and [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush"), took pro-choice positions until the early 1980s.[[454]](#cite_note-Halpern-2018-457) However, starting at this point, both George H.W. Bush and Ronald Reagan described themselves as pro-life during their presidencies.

In the 21st century, both [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush")[[457]](#cite_note-460) and [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") described themselves as "[pro-life](/wiki/Anti-abortion_movements "Anti-abortion movements")" during their terms. However, Trump stated that he supported the legality and ethics of abortion before his candidacy in 2015.[[458]](#cite_note-461)

Summarizing the rapid shift in the Republican and Democratic positions on abortion, Sue Halpern writes:[[239]](#cite_note-Williams-2022-241)

> ...in the late 1960s and early 1970s, many Republicans were behind efforts to liberalize and even decriminalize abortion; theirs was the party of reproductive choice, while Democrats, with their large Catholic constituency, were the opposition. Republican governor Ronald Reagan signed the California Therapeutic Abortion Act, one of the most liberal abortion laws in the country, in 1967, legalizing abortion for women whose mental or physical health would be impaired by pregnancy, or whose pregnancies were the result of rape or incest. The same year, the Republican strongholds of North Carolina and Colorado made it easier for women to obtain abortions. New York, under Governor [Nelson Rockefeller](/wiki/Nelson_Rockefeller "Nelson Rockefeller"), a Republican, eliminated all restrictions on women seeking to terminate pregnancies up to twenty-four weeks gestation.... Richard Nixon, Barry Goldwater, Gerald Ford, and George H.W. Bush were all pro-choice, and they were not party outliers. In 1972, a Gallup poll found that 68 percent of Republicans believed abortion to be a private matter between a woman and her doctor. The government, they said, should not be involved...

Since the 1980s, opposition to abortion has become strongest in the party among [traditionalist Catholics](/wiki/Traditionalist_Catholicism "Traditionalist Catholicism") and conservative Protestant evangelicals.[[239]](#cite_note-Williams-2022-241)[[456]](#cite_note-Taylor-2018-459)[[459]](#cite_note-Abdelfatah-2022-462) Initially, evangelicals were relatively indifferent to the cause of abortion and overwhelmingly viewed it as a concern that was [sectarian](/wiki/Sectarianism "Sectarianism") and [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[459]](#cite_note-Abdelfatah-2022-462) Historian [Randall Balmer](/wiki/Randall_Balmer "Randall Balmer") notes that [Billy Graham](/wiki/Billy_Graham "Billy Graham")'s *[Christianity Today](/wiki/Christianity_Today "Christianity Today")* published in 1968 a statement by theologian [Bruce Waltke](/wiki/Bruce_Waltke "Bruce Waltke") that:[[460]](#cite_note-463) "God does not regard the fetus as a soul, no matter how far gestation has progressed." Typical of the time, *Christianity Today* "refused to characterize abortion as sinful" and cited "individual health, family welfare, and social responsibility" as "justifications for ending a pregnancy."[[461]](#cite_note-Balmer-2022-464) Similar beliefs were held among conservative figures in the [Southern Baptist Convention](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention "Southern Baptist Convention"), including [W. A. Criswell](/wiki/W._A._Criswell "W. A. Criswell"), who is partially credited with starting the "[conservative resurgence](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention_conservative_resurgence "Southern Baptist Convention conservative resurgence")" within the organization, who stated: "I have always felt that it was only after a child was born and had a life separate from its mother that it became an individual person and it has always, therefore, seemed to me that what is best for the mother and for the future should be allowed." Balmer argues that evangelical American Christianity being inherently tied to opposition to abortion is a relatively new occurrence.[[461]](#cite_note-Balmer-2022-464)[[462]](#cite_note-465) After the late 1970s, he writes, opinion against abortion among evangelicals rapidly shifted in favor of its prohibition.[[459]](#cite_note-Abdelfatah-2022-462)

Today, opinion polls show that Republican voters are heavily divided on the legality of abortion,[[230]](#cite_note-Doherty-2023-232) although the vast majority of the party's national and state candidates are [anti-abortion](/wiki/Anti-abortion "Anti-abortion") and oppose elective abortion on religious or moral grounds. While many advocate exceptions in the case of [incest](/wiki/Incest "Incest"), rape, or the mother's life being at risk, in 2012 the party approved a platform advocating banning abortions without exception.[[463]](#cite_note-platform-466) There were not highly polarized differences between the Democratic Party and the Republican Party before the *[Roe v. Wade](/wiki/Roe_v._Wade "Roe v. Wade")* 1973 Supreme Court ruling (which made prohibitions on abortion rights unconstitutional), but after the Supreme Court ruling, opposition to abortion became an increasingly key national platform for the Republican Party.[[464]](#cite_note-The_Great_Divide:_Religious_and_Cultural_Conflict_in_American_Party_Politics2-467)[[465]](#cite_note-How_race_and_religion_have_polarized_American_voters-468)[[466]](#cite_note-469) As a result, Evangelicals gravitated towards the Republican Party.[[464]](#cite_note-The_Great_Divide:_Religious_and_Cultural_Conflict_in_American_Party_Politics2-467)[[465]](#cite_note-How_race_and_religion_have_polarized_American_voters-468) Most Republicans oppose government funding for abortion providers, notably [Planned Parenthood](/wiki/Planned_Parenthood "Planned Parenthood").[[467]](#cite_note-ontheissues-470) This includes support for the [Hyde Amendment](/wiki/Hyde_Amendment "Hyde Amendment").

Until its dissolution in 2018, [Republican Majority for Choice](/wiki/Republican_Majority_for_Choice "Republican Majority for Choice"), an abortion rights PAC, advocated for amending the GOP platform to include pro-abortion rights members.[[468]](#cite_note-471)

The Republican Party has pursued policies at the national and state levels to restrict [embryonic stem cell](/wiki/Embryonic_stem_cell "Embryonic stem cell") research beyond the original lines because it involves the destruction of human [embryos](/wiki/Embryo "Embryo").[[469]](#cite_note-472)[[470]](#cite_note-473)

After [the overturning](/wiki/Dobbs_v._Jackson_Women%27s_Health_Organization "Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization") of *Roe v. Wade* in 2022, a majority of Republican-controlled states [passed near-total bans](/wiki/Abortion_law_in_the_United_States_by_state "Abortion law in the United States by state") on abortion, rendering it largely illegal throughout much of the United States.[[471]](#cite_note-474)[[472]](#cite_note-475)

#### Affirmative action

See also: [Affirmative action in the United States](/wiki/Affirmative_action_in_the_United_States "Affirmative action in the United States")

Republicans generally oppose [affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action "Affirmative action"), often describing it as a "[quota system](/wiki/Racial_quota "Racial quota")" and believing that it is not [meritocratic](/wiki/Meritocratic "Meritocratic") and is counter-productive socially, with critics arguing that it promotes *reverse discrimination*. According to a 2023 ABC poll, a majority of Americans (52%) and 75% of Republicans supported the Supreme Court's decision in [Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard](/wiki/Students_for_Fair_Admissions_v._Harvard "Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard") prohibiting race as a factor in college admissions, compared to only 26% of Democrats.[[473]](#cite_note-476)

The 2012 Republican national platform stated, "We support efforts to help low-income individuals get a fair chance based on their potential and individual merit; but we reject preferences, quotas, and set-asides, as the best or sole methods through which fairness can be achieved, whether in government, education, or corporate boardrooms...Merit, ability, aptitude, and results should be the factors that determine advancement in our society."[[474]](#cite_note-477)[[475]](#cite_note-affirmativeaction-478)[[476]](#cite_note-Eilperin-479)[[477]](#cite_note-480)

#### Gun ownership

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/20210420_Gun_control_survey_by_political_party_-_Pew_Research.svg/250px-20210420_Gun_control_survey_by_political_party_-_Pew_Research.svg.png)](/wiki/File:20210420_Gun_control_survey_by_political_party_-_Pew_Research.svg)

A 2021 survey of U.S. opinion on gun control issues, revealing deep divides along political lines[[478]](#cite_note-Pew_20210420-481)

Republicans generally support [gun ownership rights](/wiki/Gun_politics_in_the_United_States "Gun politics in the United States") and oppose [laws regulating guns](/wiki/Gun_law_in_the_United_States "Gun law in the United States") with 23% of republicans favoring stricter gun laws compared to 86% of democrats. According to a 2023 Pew Research Center poll, 45% of Republicans and Republican-leaning independents personally own firearms, compared to 32% for the general public and 20% of Democrats and Democratic-leaning independents.[[479]](#cite_note-482)

The [National Rifle Association of America](/wiki/National_Rifle_Association_of_America "National Rifle Association of America"), a [special interest group](/wiki/Advocacy_group "Advocacy group") in support of gun ownership, has consistently aligned itself with the Republican Party.[[480]](#cite_note-483) Following gun control measures under the [Clinton administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_Bill_Clinton "Presidency of Bill Clinton"), such as the [Violent Crime Control and Law Enforcement Act of 1994](/wiki/Violent_Crime_Control_and_Law_Enforcement_Act_of_1994 "Violent Crime Control and Law Enforcement Act of 1994"), the Republicans allied with the NRA during the [Republican Revolution](/wiki/Republican_Revolution "Republican Revolution") in [1994](/wiki/1994_United_States_elections "1994 United States elections").[[481]](#cite_note-484) Since then, the NRA has consistently backed Republican candidates and contributed financial support.[[482]](#cite_note-485)

In contrast, [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush"), formerly a lifelong NRA member, was highly critical of the organization following their response to the [Oklahoma City bombing](/wiki/Oklahoma_City_bombing "Oklahoma City bombing") authored by CEO [Wayne LaPierre](/wiki/Wayne_LaPierre "Wayne LaPierre"), and publicly resigned in protest.[[483]](#cite_note-486)

#### Criminal justice

See also: [Illegal drug trade in the United States](/wiki/Illegal_drug_trade_in_the_United_States "Illegal drug trade in the United States"), [Legality of cannabis by U.S. jurisdiction](/wiki/Legality_of_cannabis_by_U.S._jurisdiction "Legality of cannabis by U.S. jurisdiction"), [Capital punishment in the United States](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_the_United_States "Capital punishment in the United States"), and [Incarceration in the United States](/wiki/Incarceration_in_the_United_States "Incarceration in the United States")

The Republican Party has generally promoted strict anti-crime policies, such as [mandatory minimum sentences](/wiki/Mandatory_minimum_sentences "Mandatory minimum sentences") and the death penalty.[[484]](#cite_note-Lancaster-2024-487)[[485]](#cite_note-488)[[486]](#cite_note-489) In the 2010s, however, prominent Republicans demonstrated some interest in [criminal justice reform](/wiki/Criminal_justice_reform_in_the_United_States "Criminal justice reform in the United States") designed to combat [mass incarceration](/wiki/Incarceration_in_the_United_States "Incarceration in the United States"), with President Trump signing the [First Step Act](/wiki/First_Step_Act "First Step Act"), which expanded good behavior credits for perpetrators of most nonviolent crimes and required the U.S. Attorney General to develop a system to assess the recidivism risk of all federal prisoners.[[487]](#cite_note-490) By 2024, however, the Republican Party and its leaders had largely left behind its prior support for reform of the justice system.[[484]](#cite_note-Lancaster-2024-487)
Republican elected officials have historically supported the [war on drugs](/wiki/War_on_drugs "War on drugs"). They generally oppose [legalization](/wiki/Legalization_of_drugs "Legalization of drugs") or decriminalization of drugs such as [marijuana](/wiki/Cannabis_in_the_United_States "Cannabis in the United States").[[488]](#cite_note-Tesler-2022-491)[[489]](#cite_note-492)[[490]](#cite_note-493)

Opposition to the legalization of marijuana has softened significantly over time among Republican voters and politicians.[[491]](#cite_note-494)[[492]](#cite_note-495)[[493]](#cite_note-496) A 2021 [*Quinnipiac*](/wiki/Quinnipiac_University "Quinnipiac University") poll found that 62% of Republicans supported the legalization of recreational marijuana use and that net support for the position was +30 points.[[488]](#cite_note-Tesler-2022-491) Some Republican-controlled states have legalized medical and recreational marijuana in recent years.[[494]](#cite_note-NCSL_MMJ-497) In September 2024, then-candidate Donald Trump endorsed the legalization of recreational marijuana.[[495]](#cite_note-498)

#### Immigration

See also: [Immigration to the United States](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States") and [Illegal immigration to the United States](/wiki/Illegal_immigration_to_the_United_States "Illegal immigration to the United States")

The Republican Party has taken widely varying views on immigration throughout its history, but has generally and traditionally taken an anti-immigration and [nativist](/wiki/Nativism_(politics) "Nativism (politics)") stance compared to the opposition.[[13]](#cite_note-Smith-2021-14) In the period between 1850 and 1870, the Republican Party was more opposed to immigration than the Democrats. The GOP's opposition was, in part, caused by its reliance on the support of anti-Catholic and anti-immigrant parties such as the [Know-Nothings](/wiki/Know_Nothing "Know Nothing"). In the decades following the Civil War, especially in the 1880s, the Republican Party lessened its stance on immigration, as it represented the manufacturers in the northeast (who wanted additional labor); although during this period, the Democratic Party still came to be seen as the party of both American and foreign labor, and many religious Republicans [used anti-Irish and pro-Christian sentiments](/wiki/Romanism "Romanism"). Starting in the early 1930s, the parties focused on [Mexican emigration](/wiki/Emigration_from_Mexico "Emigration from Mexico"), as the Democrats proposed a softer stance on Mexican immigration during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") and [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal"), rather than Republicans under [Herbert Hoover](/wiki/Herbert_Hoover "Herbert Hoover").[[496]](#cite_note-499)[[497]](#cite_note-500)

In 2006, the Republican-led Senate passed [comprehensive immigration reform](/wiki/Comprehensive_Immigration_Reform_Act_of_2006 "Comprehensive Immigration Reform Act of 2006") that would eventually have allowed millions of illegal immigrants to become citizens. Despite the support of Republican President George W. Bush, the House of Representatives (also led by Republicans) did not advance the bill.[[498]](#cite_note-Blanton-501) After Republican Mitt Romney was defeated in the 2012 presidential election, particularly due to a lack of support among Latinos,[[499]](#cite_note-Thrush_2012_z257-502)[[500]](#cite_note-Murray_2012_q346-503) several Republicans advocated a friendlier approach to immigrants that would allow for more migrant workers and a [path to citizenship](/wiki/Path_to_citizenship "Path to citizenship") for illegal immigrants. The [Border Security, Economic Opportunity, and Immigration Modernization Act of 2013](/wiki/Border_Security,_Economic_Opportunity,_and_Immigration_Modernization_Act_of_2013 "Border Security, Economic Opportunity, and Immigration Modernization Act of 2013") passed the Senate 68–32, but was not brought to a vote in the House and died in the [113th Congress](/wiki/113th_Congress "113th Congress").[[501]](#cite_note-AP_News-2023-504) In a 2013 poll, 60% of Republicans supported the pathway to citizenship concept.[[502]](#cite_note-long-past-505)

In 2016, [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") proposed to build [a wall](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border_wall "Mexico–United States border wall") along the southern border of the United States. Trump [immigration policies during his administration](/wiki/Immigration_policy_of_the_first_Donald_Trump_administration "Immigration policy of the first Donald Trump administration") included a [travel ban](/wiki/Executive_Order_13769 "Executive Order 13769") from multiple Muslim-majority countries, a [Remain in Mexico](/wiki/Remain_in_Mexico "Remain in Mexico") policy for asylum-seekers, [a controversial family separation policy](/wiki/Trump_administration_family_separation_policy "Trump administration family separation policy"), and attempting to end [DACA](/wiki/Deferred_Action_for_Childhood_Arrivals "Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals").[[171]](#cite_note-Baker-2020-173)[[503]](#cite_note-506) During the tenure of Democratic President Joe Biden, the Republican Party has continued to take a hardline stance against illegal immigration. The Party largely opposes immigration reform,[[504]](#cite_note-507) although there are widely differing views on immigration within the Party.[[501]](#cite_note-AP_News-2023-504) The Party's proposed 2024 platform was opposed to immigration, and called for the mass deportation of all illegal immigrants in the United States,[[505]](#cite_note-2024_Platform-508) which during Trump's second presidency was extended to immigrants without permanent legal status.[[506]](#cite_note-509) A 2024 Pew Research Center poll found that 88% of Donald Trump's supporters favored mass deportation of all illegal immigrants, compared to 27% of Kamala Harris supporters.[[507]](#cite_note-510)

#### LGBT issues

Similar to the Democratic Party, the Republican position on [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBT rights in the United States") has changed significantly over time, with continuously increasing support among both parties on the issue.[[508]](#cite_note-Lindberg-2022-511)[[509]](#cite_note-Igielnik-2022-512) The [Log Cabin Republicans](/wiki/Log_Cabin_Republicans "Log Cabin Republicans") is a group within the Republican Party that represents [LGBT conservatives](/wiki/LGBT_conservatism "LGBT conservatism") and allies and advocates for LGBT rights.[[510]](#cite_note-m543-513)[[511]](#cite_note-e209-514)

From the early 2000s to the mid-2010s, Republicans opposed [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage"), while being divided on the issue of [civil unions](/wiki/Civil_union "Civil union") and [domestic partnerships](/wiki/Domestic_partnership "Domestic partnership") for same-sex couples.[[512]](#cite_note-515) During the 2004 election, [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") campaigned prominently on a constitutional amendment to prohibit same-sex marriage; many believe it helped Bush win re-election.[[513]](#cite_note-Lerer-2020-516)[[514]](#cite_note-517) In both [2004](/wiki/108th_United_States_Congress "108th United States Congress")[[515]](#cite_note-518) and [2006](/wiki/109th_United_States_Congress "109th United States Congress"),[[516]](#cite_note-519) President Bush, Senate Majority Leader [Bill Frist](/wiki/Bill_Frist "Bill Frist"), and House Majority Leader [John Boehner](/wiki/John_Boehner "John Boehner") promoted the [Federal Marriage Amendment](/wiki/Federal_Marriage_Amendment "Federal Marriage Amendment"), a proposed constitutional amendment which would legally restrict the definition of marriage to [heterosexual](/wiki/Heterosexuality "Heterosexuality") couples.[[517]](#cite_note-520)[[518]](#cite_note-521)[[519]](#cite_note-522) In both attempts, the amendment failed to secure enough votes to invoke [cloture](/wiki/Cloture "Cloture") and thus ultimately was never passed. As more states legalized same-sex marriage in the 2010s, Republicans increasingly supported allowing each state to decide its own marriage policy.[[520]](#cite_note-A_Shifting_Landscape-523) As of 2014, most state GOP platforms expressed opposition to same-sex marriage.[[521]](#cite_note-524) The 2016 [GOP Platform](/wiki/2016_Republican_National_Convention "2016 Republican National Convention") defined marriage as "natural marriage, the union of one man and one woman," and condemned the Supreme Court's [ruling](/wiki/Obergefell_v._Hodges "Obergefell v. Hodges") legalizing same-sex marriages.[[522]](#cite_note-525)[[523]](#cite_note-526) The 2020 platform, which reused the 2016 platform, retained the statements against same-sex marriage.[[524]](#cite_note-527)[[525]](#cite_note-528)[[526]](#cite_note-529)

Following his election as president in 2016, Donald Trump stated that he had no objection to same-sex marriage or to the Supreme Court decision in *[Obergefell v. Hodges](/wiki/Obergefell_v._Hodges "Obergefell v. Hodges")*, but had previously promised to consider appointing a Supreme Court justice to roll back the constitutional right.[[513]](#cite_note-Lerer-2020-516)[[527]](#cite_note-530) In office, Trump was the first sitting Republican president to recognize [LGBT Pride Month](/wiki/Gay_pride "Gay pride").[[528]](#cite_note-531) Conversely, the Trump administration banned transgender individuals from service in the United States military and rolled back other protections for transgender people which had been enacted during the previous Democratic presidency.[[529]](#cite_note-532) However, other Republicans, such as [Vivek Ramaswamy](/wiki/Vivek_Ramaswamy "Vivek Ramaswamy"), do not support such a ban.[[530]](#cite_note-533)

The Republican Party platform previously opposed the [inclusion of gay people in the military](/wiki/Sexual_orientation_and_the_United_States_military "Sexual orientation and the United States military") and opposed adding sexual orientation to the list of protected classes since 1992.[[531]](#cite_note-534)[[532]](#cite_note-535)[[533]](#cite_note-536) The Republican Party opposed the inclusion of [sexual preference](/wiki/Sexual_preference "Sexual preference") in anti-discrimination statutes from 1992 to 2004.[[534]](#cite_note-1992_Republican_Party_platform-537) The 2008 and 2012 Republican Party platform supported anti-discrimination statutes based on sex, race, age, religion, creed, disability, or national origin, but both platforms were silent on [sexual orientation](/wiki/Sexual_orientation "Sexual orientation") and [gender identity](/wiki/Gender_identity "Gender identity").[[535]](#cite_note-2012_Republican_Party_platform-538)[[536]](#cite_note-2008_Republican_Party_platform-539) The 2016 platform was opposed to sex discrimination statutes that included the phrase "sexual orientation".[[537]](#cite_note-540)[[538]](#cite_note-541) The same 2016 platform rejected *Obergefell v. Hodges*, and was also used for the party's 2020 platform.[[539]](#cite_note-Zezima_Weigel_2016_d265-542) In the early 2020s, numerous Republican-led states [proposed or passed laws](/wiki/2020s_anti-LGBT_movement_in_the_United_States "2020s anti-LGBT movement in the United States") that have been described as anti-trans by critics,[[540]](#cite_note-x150-543)[[541]](#cite_note-n141-544)[[542]](#cite_note-v293-545)[[543]](#cite_note-o776-546)[[544]](#cite_note-l264-547)[[545]](#cite_note-j502-548)[[546]](#cite_note-v746-549) as well as laws limiting or banning [public performances of drag shows](/wiki/Drag_panic "Drag panic"), and teaching schoolchildren about LGBT topics.[[541]](#cite_note-n141-544)

On November 6, 2021, RNC Chair [Ronna McDaniel](/wiki/Ronna_McDaniel "Ronna McDaniel") announced the creation of the "RNC Pride Coalition", in partnership with the [Log Cabin Republicans](/wiki/Log_Cabin_Republicans "Log Cabin Republicans"), to promote outreach to LGBTQ voters.[[547]](#cite_note-550) However, after the announcement, McDaniel apologized for not having communicated the announcement in advance and emphasized that the new outreach program did not alter the 2016 GOP Platform.[[548]](#cite_note-551)

As of 2023, a majority of Republican voters support [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_the_United_States "Same-sex marriage in the United States").[[508]](#cite_note-Lindberg-2022-511)[[549]](#cite_note-552)[[550]](#cite_note-553) According to *[FiveThirtyEight](/wiki/FiveThirtyEight "FiveThirtyEight")*, as of 2022, Republican voters are consistently more open to same-sex marriage than their representatives.[[551]](#cite_note-554)[[552]](#cite_note-555) The party platform approved at the [2024 Republican National Convention](/wiki/2024_Republican_National_Convention "2024 Republican National Convention") no longer states that marriage should be between "one man and one woman", though it did oppose the inclusion of [transgender women](/wiki/Trans_woman "Trans woman") in women's sports and teaching about LGBT topics in schools.[[505]](#cite_note-2024_Platform-508) According to a 2023 [YouGov](/wiki/YouGov "YouGov") poll, Republicans are slightly more likely to oppose [intersex medical alterations](/wiki/Intersex_medical_interventions "Intersex medical interventions") than Democrats.[[553]](#cite_note-556)[[554]](#cite_note-557)

In November 2024, Trump nominated [Scott Bessent](/wiki/Scott_Bessent "Scott Bessent") for [United States Secretary of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Secretary of the Treasury").[[555]](#cite_note-558) He is the [second openly gay man](/wiki/List_of_first_openly_LGBTQ_politicians_in_the_United_States "List of first openly LGBTQ politicians in the United States") to serve in the [Cabinet of the United States](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_States "Cabinet of the United States") (after [Pete Buttigieg](/wiki/Pete_Buttigieg "Pete Buttigieg")) and the [fourth openly gay man](/wiki/List_of_first_openly_LGBTQ_politicians_in_the_United_States "List of first openly LGBTQ politicians in the United States") to serve in a [cabinet-level](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_States#Cabinet-level_officials "Cabinet of the United States") office (after [Demetrios Marantis](/wiki/Demetrios_Marantis "Demetrios Marantis"), [Richard Grenell](/wiki/Richard_Grenell "Richard Grenell") and Buttigieg).[[556]](#cite_note-559) As the secretary of the treasury is [fifth](/wiki/United_States_presidential_line_of_succession#Current_order_of_succession "United States presidential line of succession") in the [United States presidential line of succession](/wiki/United_States_presidential_line_of_succession "United States presidential line of succession"), he is the [highest-ranking openly LGBT person in American history](/wiki/List_of_first_openly_LGBTQ_politicians_in_the_United_States "List of first openly LGBTQ politicians in the United States").[[557]](#cite_note-560)

#### Voting rights

See also: [Voter identification laws in the United States](/wiki/Voter_identification_laws_in_the_United_States "Voter identification laws in the United States")

Virtually all restrictions on voting have in recent years been implemented by Republicans. Republicans, mainly at the state level, argue that the restrictions (such as the purging of [voter rolls](/wiki/Electoral_roll "Electoral roll"), limiting voting locations, and limiting [early](/wiki/Early_voting "Early voting") and [mail-in](/wiki/Postal_voting "Postal voting") voting) are vital to prevent [voter fraud](/wiki/Voter_fraud "Voter fraud"), saying that voter fraud is an underestimated issue in elections. Polling has found majority support for early voting, automatic voter registration, and voter ID laws among the general population.[[558]](#cite_note-561)[[559]](#cite_note-562)[[560]](#cite_note-563)

In defending their restrictions to voting rights, Republicans have made false and exaggerated claims about the extent of voter fraud in the United States; all existing research indicates that it is extremely rare,[[561]](#cite_note-WaPo_voter_fraud_2014-564)[[562]](#cite_note-565)[[563]](#cite_note-Hakim-2018-566)[[564]](#cite_note-Linker-2018-567) and civil and voting rights organizations often accuse Republicans of enacting restrictions to influence elections in the party's favor. Many laws or regulations restricting voting enacted by Republicans have been successfully challenged in court, with court rulings striking down such regulations and accusing Republicans of establishing them with a partisan purpose.[[563]](#cite_note-Hakim-2018-566)[[564]](#cite_note-Linker-2018-567)

After the Supreme Court decision in *[Shelby County v. Holder](/wiki/Shelby_County_v._Holder "Shelby County v. Holder")* rolled back aspects of the [Voting Rights Act of 1965](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act_of_1965 "Voting Rights Act of 1965"), Republicans introduced cuts to early voting, purges of voter rolls, and imposition of strict voter ID laws.[[563]](#cite_note-Hakim-2018-566) The 2016 Republican platform advocated proof of citizenship as a prerequisite for registering to vote and photo ID as a prerequisite when voting.[[565]](#cite_note-568)

After Donald Trump and his [Republican allies made false claims of fraud](/wiki/Republican_reactions_to_Donald_Trump%27s_claims_of_2020_election_fraud "Republican reactions to Donald Trump's claims of 2020 election fraud") during the 2020 presidential election, Republicans launched a nationwide effort to [impose tighter election laws at the state level](/wiki/Republican_efforts_to_restrict_voting_following_the_2020_presidential_election "Republican efforts to restrict voting following the 2020 presidential election").[[566]](#cite_note-569)[[567]](#cite_note-570)[[568]](#cite_note-Gardner-2021-571) Such bills are centered around limiting mail-in voting, strengthening voter ID laws, shortening early voting, eliminating [automatic](/wiki/Voter_registration_in_the_United_States#Automatic "Voter registration in the United States") and [same-day voter registration](/wiki/Voter_registration_in_the_United_States#Election_Day_/_same-day "Voter registration in the United States"), curbing the use of [ballot drop boxes](/wiki/Ballot_drop_box "Ballot drop box"), and allowing for increased purging of voter rolls.[[569]](#cite_note-Bill-Tracker-572)[[570]](#cite_note-NYT-practices-573) Republicans in at least eight states have also introduced bills that would give lawmakers greater power over election administration, after they were unsuccessful in their attempts to overturn election results in [swing states](/wiki/Swing_state "Swing state") won by Biden.[[571]](#cite_note-Corasaniti-2021-574)[[568]](#cite_note-Gardner-2021-571)[[572]](#cite_note-575)[[573]](#cite_note-576)

Supporters of the bills argue they would improve election security and reverse temporary changes enacted during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"); they point to false claims of significant election fraud, as well as the substantial public distrust of the integrity of the 2020 election those claims have fostered,[[d]](#cite_note-579) as justification.[[576]](#cite_note-580)[[577]](#cite_note-581)[[578]](#cite_note-582) Political analysts say that the efforts amount to [voter suppression](/wiki/Voter_suppression "Voter suppression"), are intended to advantage Republicans by reducing the number of people who vote, and would disproportionately affect [minority](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States "Race and ethnicity in the United States") voters.[[579]](#cite_note-583)[[580]](#cite_note-584)[[581]](#cite_note-585)[[582]](#cite_note-586)

## Composition and demographics

According to a 2025 [Gallup poll](/wiki/Gallup_poll "Gallup poll"), 46% of Americans identify or lean towards Republicans, and 45% identify or lean towards Democrats. Republicans have held an edge since 2022, while the Democratic Party had previously held an overall edge in party identification from 1992 to 2021, since Gallup began polling on the issue in 1991.[[583]](#cite_note-587) In 2016, *The New York Times* stated that the party was strongest in the [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"), most of the [Midwestern](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") and [Mountain States](/wiki/Mountain_States "Mountain States"), and [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska").[[584]](#cite_note-588)

The Republican party's core voting demographics are White voters without college degrees and [White Southerners](/wiki/White_Southerners "White Southerners").[[585]](#cite_note-White_Voters-589) Racial polarization is extremely high in the Southern United States, with [White Southerners](/wiki/White_Southerners "White Southerners") almost entirely voting for the Republican Party and [Black Southerners](/wiki/Black_Southerners "Black Southerners") almost entirely voting for the Democratic Party.[[586]](#cite_note-Cohn-2014-590)

As of 2024, the Republican Party has support from a majority of [White](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans")[[587]](#cite_note-Staff-2024-591) voters, and increasingly among [Hispanics](/wiki/Hispanic "Hispanic")[[66]](#cite_note-Lange-2024-68) and [Asians](/wiki/Asian_people "Asian people").[[165]](#cite_note-asian_americans_5_points-167)

A majority of [working-class](/wiki/Working_class_in_the_United_States "Working class in the United States"),[[66]](#cite_note-Lange-2024-68) [rural](/wiki/Urban%E2%80%93rural_political_divide "Urban–rural political divide"),[[95]](#cite_note-Maxwell_2019-97)[[129]](#cite_note-The_World_Trump_Wants-131) men,[[587]](#cite_note-Staff-2024-591) individuals without [college degrees](/wiki/Educational_attainment_in_the_United_States "Educational attainment in the United States"),[[587]](#cite_note-Staff-2024-591) and [lower income](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country")[[588]](#cite_note-Suss-2024-592) voters vote for the party. Traditionalist religious voters,[[589]](#cite_note-593) including [Evangelicals](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism")[[587]](#cite_note-Staff-2024-591) [Latter-Day Saints](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saint_movement "Latter Day Saint movement"), [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims"),[[590]](#cite_note-Aleaziz-2024-594) and [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_the_United_States "Catholic Church in the United States")[[587]](#cite_note-Staff-2024-591) voters lean towards the Republicans.[[159]](#cite_note-Polarization_by_education-161)[[155]](#cite_note-cambridge.org-157) The party has made significant gains among the [white working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class"),[[66]](#cite_note-Lange-2024-68) [Asians](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"),[[591]](#cite_note-Yam-2024-595) and [Hispanics](/wiki/Hispanic "Hispanic").[[587]](#cite_note-Staff-2024-591)

Republicans have lost support among [upper middle class](/wiki/Upper_middle_class_in_the_United_States "Upper middle class in the United States") and [college-educated](/wiki/Postgraduate_education "Postgraduate education") whites.[[160]](#cite_note-Nate_Silver-162)[[588]](#cite_note-Suss-2024-592)[[592]](#cite_note-Levitz-2022-596) In 2024, Trump only narrowly won White voters making $100,000 to $199,999 (50–49%), over $200,000 (51–48%), and White men with college degrees (50-48%), all on par with Trump winning the popular vote 50–48%.[[156]](#cite_note-Harry_Enten-158)

### Income

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Median_Household_Income_per_County_as_of_2021_according_to_the_USDA_Economic_Research_Service_%283%29.png/330px-Median_Household_Income_per_County_as_of_2021_according_to_the_USDA_Economic_Research_Service_%283%29.png)](/wiki/File:Median_Household_Income_per_County_as_of_2021_according_to_the_USDA_Economic_Research_Service_(3).png)

Median U.S. household income per county in 2021, showing the distribution of income geographically in the United States

Until 2016, higher income was strongly correlated with voting for the Republican Party among the general electorate. However, in all three of Trump's elections in 2016, 2020, and 2024, the previous correlation between higher incomes and voting for the Republican Party was largely eliminated among the electorate as a whole.[[212]](#cite_note-culture_trumps_economic_class-214) For White voters, instead higher educational attainment was strongly correlated with higher support for the Democratic Party.[[156]](#cite_note-Harry_Enten-158) According to a 2024 Pew Research Center poll, homeowners are slightly more likely to be Republicans (51-45%), while renters are much more likely to be Democrats (64-32%).[[593]](#cite_note-597)

In the 2024 presidential election, Trump did better among lower-income voters than high-income voters, the first time for the Republican nominee in modern American political history.[[588]](#cite_note-Suss-2024-592) Trump lost voters making annual incomes over $100,000 (47–51%) and $200,000 (46–52%) to Democrat [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris"), with voters making over $200,000 a year being Trump's weakest income demographic. Trump won voters making less than $100,000 (51–47%) and $50,000 (50–48%), though Trump did lose voters making less than $30,000 (46–50%).[[214]](#cite_note-Exit_poll_results_2024-216)

Trump won some of the [lowest-income counties](/wiki/List_of_lowest-income_counties_in_the_United_States "List of lowest-income counties in the United States"), mainly majority-White counties in [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia").[[594]](#cite_note-598) Most of the lowest-income counties are [majority-Black counties](/wiki/List_of_majority-Black_counties_in_the_United_States "List of majority-Black counties in the United States") in the [Southern Black Belt](/wiki/Black_Belt_in_the_American_South "Black Belt in the American South"), which Trump lost.[[595]](#cite_note-599)

Men without college degrees, particularly [blue-collar](/wiki/Blue-collar_worker "Blue-collar worker") men, are Donald Trump's strongest demographic. Per exit polls, Trump won White men without college degrees (69-29%) and just over half of Hispanic men in the 2024 presidential election.[[23]](#cite_note-gap_within_the_gender_gap-25)

### Region

Main article: [Solid South](/wiki/Solid_South "Solid South")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/BibleBelt.png/330px-BibleBelt.png)](/wiki/File:BibleBelt.png)

Approximate boundaries of the Bible Belt

Some of the oldest Republican strongholds in the country are in the Southern United States, particularly majority-White [Unionist](/wiki/Southern_Unionist "Southern Unionist") counties in [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia").[[596]](#cite_note-temple-600) The Republican Party gradually gained power in the Southern United States since [1964](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election"). Although [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") carried 49 states in [1972](/wiki/1972_United_States_presidential_election "1972 United States presidential election"), including every Southern state, the Republican Party remained quite weak at the local and state levels across the entire South for decades. Republicans first won a majority of U.S. House seats in the South in the [1994](/wiki/1994_United_States_elections "1994 United States elections") "[Republican Revolution](/wiki/Republican_Revolution "Republican Revolution")", and only began to dominate the South after the [2010 elections](/wiki/2010_United_States_elections "2010 United States elections").[[597]](#cite_note-The_long_goodbye-601)

Since the 2010s, [White Southerners](/wiki/White_Southerners "White Southerners") are the Republican Party's strongest racial demographic, in some [Deep South](/wiki/Deep_South "Deep South") states voting nearly as Republican as African Americans vote Democratic.[[586]](#cite_note-Cohn-2014-590) This is partially attributable to religiosity, with White [evangelical Christians](/wiki/Evangelical_Christianity "Evangelical Christianity") in the [Bible Belt](/wiki/Bible_Belt "Bible Belt"), which covers most of the South, being the Republican Party's strongest religious demographic.[[585]](#cite_note-White_Voters-589) In particular, in 2024 Trump won every state with a significant presence in the Bible Belt except Virginia, because [Northern Virginia](/wiki/Northern_Virginia "Northern Virginia") is part of the heavily Democratic [Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area").[[598]](#cite_note-602)[[599]](#cite_note-Bible_Belt-603)

White Southerners with college degrees remain strongly Republican. In 2024, Trump won White Southerners 67-32%, including White Southerners with college degrees 57-41%. Trump won White evangelicals 82-17%, including White evangelicals with college degrees 75-23%.[[585]](#cite_note-White_Voters-589)

### Age

The Republican Party does best with [middle age](/wiki/Middle_age "Middle age") and [older](/wiki/Old_age "Old age") voters, particularly voters over the age of 50. In the 2024 presidential election, Trump lost voters aged 18–29 (43–54%) and 30–39 (45–51%), tied with voters aged 40–49 (49-49%), did best among voters aged 50–64 (54-44%), and narrowly won voters 65 and older (50-49%). This also holds when controlling for race.[[214]](#cite_note-Exit_poll_results_2024-216)

* Trump tied among Whites aged 18–29 (49-49%), and won Whites aged 30–44 (54-44%), 45–64 (61-37%), and 65 and older (56-43%).
* There was little difference among Black voters, with Trump losing Black voters aged 18–29 (16–83%), 30–44 (15–83%), 45–64 (14–84%), and particularly Black voters 65 and older (6–93%).
* Trump narrowly lost Hispanic voters aged 18–29 (45–51%) and 30–44 (45–52%), narrowly won Hispanic voters aged 45–64 (51-48%), and lost Hispanic voters 65 and older (41-58%).

### Gender

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/2021_Median_wealth_by_marital_status_-_US.svg/250px-2021_Median_wealth_by_marital_status_-_US.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2021_Median_wealth_by_marital_status_-_US.svg)

The median wealth of married couples exceeds that of single individuals, regardless of gender and across all age categories.[[600]](#cite_note-604)

Since 1980, a "gender gap" has seen stronger support for the Republican Party among men than among women. Unmarried and divorced women were far more likely to vote for Democrat [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry") than for Republican [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") in the 2004 presidential election.[[601]](#cite_note-wvwv2004-605) Exit polls from the 2012 elections revealed a continued weakness among unmarried women for the GOP, a large and growing portion of the electorate.[[602]](#cite_note-606) Although women supported Obama over [Mitt Romney](/wiki/Mitt_Romney "Mitt Romney") by a margin of 55–44% in 2012, Romney prevailed amongst married women, 53–46%.[[603]](#cite_note-607) Obama won unmarried women 67–31%.[[604]](#cite_note-608)

However, according to a December 2019 study, "White women are the only group of female voters who support Republican Party candidates for president. They have done so by a majority in all but 2 of the last 18 elections".[[605]](#cite_note-609)[[606]](#cite_note-610)

### Education

See also: [Educational attainment in the United States](/wiki/Educational_attainment_in_the_United_States "Educational attainment in the United States")

[![Map of the Non-college White vote in the 2020 presidential election by state.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Non-College_White_vote_by_state.jpg/330px-Non-College_White_vote_by_state.jpg)](/wiki/File:Non-College_White_vote_by_state.jpg)

[![Map of the College White vote in the 2020 presidential election by state.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/College_white_vote_by_state.jpg/330px-College_white_vote_by_state.jpg)](/wiki/File:College_white_vote_by_state.jpg)

**Top to bottom:**

Non-College and College White vote in the [2020 presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election") by state. A key for approximate margins is provided.[[607]](#cite_note-White_Vote_and_Educational_Polarization-611)

In all three of Donald Trump's elections in 2016, 2020, and 2024, for White voters lower educational attainment was strongly correlated with higher support for Trump.[[160]](#cite_note-Nate_Silver-162)[[156]](#cite_note-Harry_Enten-158)[[608]](#cite_note-Higher_Education-612) When controlling for educational attainment among White voters, there remain large variations by state and region. In particular, college-educated [White Southerners](/wiki/White_Southerners "White Southerners") remain strongly Republican.[[607]](#cite_note-White_Vote_and_Educational_Polarization-611)

The Republican Party has steadily increased the percentage of votes it receives from White voters without college degrees since the 1970s, while the [educational attainment](/wiki/Educational_attainment_in_the_United_States "Educational attainment in the United States") of the United States has steadily increased.[[155]](#cite_note-cambridge.org-157) White voters without college degrees are more likely to live in rural areas.[[609]](#cite_note-613)[[610]](#cite_note-614)

Voters with college degrees as a whole were a Republican-voting group until the 1990s. Despite losing in a landslide, Republican nominee [Barry Goldwater](/wiki/Barry_Goldwater "Barry Goldwater") nearly won a majority of voters with college degrees 48–52% in [1964](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election").[[158]](#cite_note-auto-160) Republican president [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford") won voters with college degrees 55-43% in [1976](/wiki/1976_United_States_presidential_election "1976 United States presidential election"), while narrowly losing to [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter").[[611]](#cite_note-1976_Presidential_Election_Data-615) Since the 1990s, a majority of voters with graduate degrees have consistently voted for the Democratic Party. For example, [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") won voters with just a bachelor's degree 52-46% while losing voters with a graduate degree 44–55%, while winning re-election in [2004](/wiki/2004_United_States_presidential_election "2004 United States presidential election").[[612]](#cite_note-616)

Until 2016, White voters with college degrees were a Republican-leaning group.[[159]](#cite_note-Polarization_by_education-161) Despite Obama's decisive [2008](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election") victory, Republican nominee [John McCain](/wiki/John_McCain "John McCain") won a majority of White voters with college degrees 51-47% and White voters without college degrees 58-40%.[[613]](#cite_note-617) In [2012](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election "2012 United States presidential election"), Republican nominee [Mitt Romney](/wiki/Mitt_Romney "Mitt Romney") won White voters with college degrees 56-42%, though Obama won voters with college degrees as a whole 50-48% while winning re-election.[[614]](#cite_note-618) Since the 2010s,[[159]](#cite_note-Polarization_by_education-161) White voters with college degrees have been increasingly voting for the Democratic Party.[[592]](#cite_note-Levitz-2022-596)[[615]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-619) Following the 2016 presidential election, exit polls indicated that "Donald Trump attracted a large share of the vote from Whites without a college degree, receiving 72 percent of the White non-college male vote and 62 percent of the White non-college female vote." Overall, 52% of voters with college degrees voted for Hillary Clinton in 2016, while 52% of voters without college degrees voted for Trump.[[616]](#cite_note-620)

In the [2020 United States presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election"), Donald Trump won White voters without college degrees 67-32%, while losing White voters with a college degree 48–51%.[[617]](#cite_note-nymag.com-621)[[615]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-619)[[157]](#cite_note-cnn2020PresidentialResults-159) In the [2024 United States presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"), Trump maintained his margins among White voters without college degrees 66-32% and lost White voters with a college degree 45–52%. In 2024, Trump won 56% of voters without a college degree, compared to 42% of voters with a college degree.[[214]](#cite_note-Exit_poll_results_2024-216)

### Ethnicity

See also: [Race and ethnicity in the United States](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States "Race and ethnicity in the United States")

[![White vote in the 2020 presidential election by state](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/White_vote_by_state_margin.jpg/330px-White_vote_by_state_margin.jpg)](/wiki/File:White_vote_by_state_margin.jpg)

**Top to bottom:**

White vote in the [2020 presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election") by state and county. A key for approximate margins is provided for states, while the county map uses [binary classification](/wiki/Binary_classification "Binary classification").[[585]](#cite_note-White_Voters-589)[[586]](#cite_note-Cohn-2014-590)

Republicans have consistently won the White vote in every presidential election after the [1964 presidential election](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election").[[618]](#cite_note-622) There exist large variations among White voters by region and state. In particular, Republicans lose White voters in the [Northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"), parts of the [Upper Midwest](/wiki/Upper_Midwest "Upper Midwest") and [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States").[[585]](#cite_note-White_Voters-589) Republicans are strongest with [White Southerners](/wiki/White_Southerners "White Southerners"), particularly White [evangelical Christians](/wiki/Evangelical_Christianity "Evangelical Christianity") in the [Bible Belt](/wiki/Bible_Belt "Bible Belt"), which covers most of the Southern United States. White Southerners with college degrees remain strongly Republican. In some [Deep South](/wiki/Deep_South "Deep South") states, Whites vote nearly as Republican as African Americans vote Democratic. In the 2024 presidential election, Trump won White Southerners 67-32%.[[586]](#cite_note-Cohn-2014-590)

Republicans have been winning under 15% of the African American vote in national elections since 1980. Until the [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") of the 1930s, Black people supported the Republican Party by large margins.[[619]](#cite_note-South-623) Black delegates were a sizable share of southern delegates to the national Republican convention from Reconstruction until the start of the 20th century when their share began to decline.[[620]](#cite_note-624) Black people shifted in large margins to the Democratic Party in the 1930s, when Black politicians such as Arthur Mitchell and William Dawson supported the New Deal because it would better serve the interest of Black Americans.[[621]](#cite_note-625) Black voters would become one of the core components of the [New Deal coalition](/wiki/New_Deal_coalition "New Deal coalition"). In the South, after the [Voting Rights Act](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act "Voting Rights Act") to prohibit racial discrimination in elections was passed by a bipartisan coalition in 1965, Black people were able to vote again and ever since have formed a significant portion (20–50%) of the Democratic vote in that region.[[622]](#cite_note-Sitkoff-626)

In the 2010 elections, two African American Republicans, [Tim Scott](/wiki/Tim_Scott "Tim Scott") and [Allen West](/wiki/Allen_West_(politician) "Allen West (politician)"), were elected to the House of Representatives. As of January 2023, there are four African-American Republicans in the House of Representatives and one African-American Republican in the United States Senate.[[623]](#cite_note-Holmes2010-627) In recent decades, Republicans have been moderately successful in gaining support from [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic "Hispanic") and Asian American voters. George W. Bush, who campaigned energetically for Hispanic votes, received 35% of their vote in 2000 and 44% in 2004.[[624]](#cite_note-628)[[625]](#cite_note-629)[[626]](#cite_note-2004cnnexitpolls-630) The party's strong anti-communist stance has made it popular among some minority groups from current and former Communist states, in particular [Cuban Americans](/wiki/Cuban_American "Cuban American"), [Korean Americans](/wiki/Korean_American "Korean American"), [Chinese Americans](/wiki/Chinese_Americans "Chinese Americans") and [Vietnamese Americans](/wiki/Vietnamese_American "Vietnamese American"). The 2007 election of [Bobby Jindal](/wiki/Bobby_Jindal "Bobby Jindal") as Governor of Louisiana was hailed as pathbreaking.[[627]](#cite_note-BBC7412-631) Jindal became the first elected minority governor in [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana") and the first state governor of [Indian](/wiki/Non-resident_Indian_and_person_of_Indian_origin "Non-resident Indian and person of Indian origin") descent.[[628]](#cite_note-deccanherald-632)

Republicans have gained support among racial and ethnic minorities, particularly among those who are working class, Hispanic or Latino, or Asian American since the 2010s.[[629]](#cite_note-633)[[630]](#cite_note-Teixeira-2022-634)[[631]](#cite_note-Cohn-2022-635)[[632]](#cite_note-Zitner-2022-636)[[633]](#cite_note-Kraushaar-2022a-637)[[634]](#cite_note-Kraushaar-2022b-638) According to [John Avlon](/wiki/John_Avlon "John Avlon"), in 2013, the Republican party was more ethnically diverse at the statewide elected official level than the Democratic Party was; GOP statewide elected officials included Latino Nevada Governor [Brian Sandoval](/wiki/Brian_Sandoval "Brian Sandoval") and African-American U.S. senator [Tim Scott](/wiki/Tim_Scott "Tim Scott") of South Carolina.[[635]](#cite_note-639)

In the [2008 presidential election](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election"), Republican presidential candidate [John McCain](/wiki/John_McCain "John McCain") won 55% of White votes, 35% of Asian votes, 31% of Hispanic votes, and 4% of African American votes.[[636]](#cite_note-pewresearch-640) In 2012, 88% of Romney voters were White while 56% of Obama voters were White.[[637]](#cite_note-641) In the [2024 presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"), Trump won 57% of White voters, 46% of Hispanic voters, 39% of Asian voters, and 13% of African American voters.[[214]](#cite_note-Exit_poll_results_2024-216)

[Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") won the popular vote in the [2024 United States presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election") as White voters without college degrees still strongly backed him, in addition to the gains made with Asian and Latino voters in comparison to the [2020 United States presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election"). As a whole, 84% of Trump voters were White.[[638]](#cite_note-642)

### Religious communities

Main articles: [Religion and politics in the United States](/wiki/Religion_and_politics_in_the_United_States "Religion and politics in the United States") and [Bible Belt](/wiki/Bible_Belt "Bible Belt")

See also: [The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints and politics in the United States](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints_and_politics_in_the_United_States "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints and politics in the United States")

Religion has always played a major role for both parties, but in the course of a century, the parties' religious compositions have changed. Religion was a major dividing line between the parties before [1960](/wiki/1960_United_States_presidential_election "1960 United States presidential election"), with Catholics, Jews, and southern Protestants heavily Democratic and northeastern Protestants heavily Republican. Most of the old differences faded away after the realignment of the 1970s and 1980s that undercut the New Deal coalition.[[639]](#cite_note-643) Since 1980, a large majority of [evangelicals](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") has voted Republican; 70–80% voted for Bush in 2000 and 2004 and 70% for Republican House candidates in [2006](/wiki/United_States_general_elections,_2006 "United States general elections, 2006").

Members of the Mormon faith had a mixed relationship with Donald Trump during his tenure, despite 67% of them voting for him in [2016](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election") and 56% of them supporting his presidency in [2018](/wiki/2018_United_States_elections "2018 United States elections"), disapproving of his personal behavior such as that shown during the [*Access Hollywood* controversy](/wiki/Donald_Trump_Access_Hollywood_tape "Donald Trump Access Hollywood tape").[[640]](#cite_note-644) In the [2020 United States presidential election in Utah](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election_in_Utah "2020 United States presidential election in Utah"), Trump won the state by about 21.5%, by a margin more than 20% lower compared to Mitt Romney (who is Mormon) in [2012](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election_in_Utah "2012 United States presidential election in Utah") and George W. Bush in [2004](/wiki/2004_United_States_presidential_election_in_Utah "2004 United States presidential election in Utah"). Their opinion on Trump had not affected their party affiliation, however, as 76% of Mormons in 2018 expressed a preference for generic Republican congressional candidates.[[641]](#cite_note-645) Similarly, while Trump again won majority-Mormon [Utah in 2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election_in_Utah "2024 United States presidential election in Utah"), the state had one of the smallest swings to the right and Trump's 22% margin was well below that of prior Republican presidential nominees.[[642]](#cite_note-646)

Jews continue to vote 70–80% Democratic; however, a slim majority of [Orthodox Jews](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") voted for the Republican Party in 2016, following years of growing Orthodox Jewish support for the party due to its social conservatism and increasingly pro-Israel foreign policy stance.[[643]](#cite_note-Sales-Adkins-2020-647) Over 70% of Orthodox Jews identify as Republican or Republican-leaning as of 2021.[[644]](#cite_note-648) An exit poll conducted by the [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") for 2020 found 35% of [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") voted for Donald Trump.[[645]](#cite_note-649) The [mainline Protestants](/wiki/Mainline_Protestants "Mainline Protestants") have dropped to about 55% Republican (in contrast to 75% before 1968). Democrats have close links with the African American churches, especially the [National Baptists](/wiki/National_Baptist_Convention,_USA,_Inc. "National Baptist Convention, USA, Inc."), while their historic dominance among Catholic voters has eroded to 54–46 in the 2010 midterms.[[646]](#cite_note-650)

Although once strongly Democratic, [American Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_the_United_States "Catholic Church in the United States") voters have been politically divided in the 21st century with 52% of Catholic voters voting for Trump in [2016](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election") and 52% voting for Biden in [2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election"). While Catholic Republican leaders try to stay in line with the teachings of the Catholic Church on subjects such as abortion, contraception, euthanasia, and embryonic stem cell research, they tend to differ on the death penalty and same-sex marriage.[[647]](#cite_note-651)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-12)** Includes [Trumpism](/wiki/Trumpism "Trumpism")[[9]](#cite_note-Ball_2024-9)[[10]](#cite_note-v075-10)[[11]](#cite_note-i277-11)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-20)**  217 representatives are members of the party; however, one [independent](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician") member, [Kevin Kiley](/wiki/Kevin_Kiley_(politician) "Kevin Kiley (politician)"), caucuses with the Republicans.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-364)** Right-to-work laws ban [union security agreements](/wiki/Union_security_agreement "Union security agreement"), which require all workers in a unionized workplace to pay dues or a fair-share fee regardless of whether they are members of the union or not.[[361]](#cite_note-363)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-579)** According to an NPR/PBS NewsHour/Marist poll, while more than 60% of Americans believe the 2020 election was secure, a large majority of Republican voters say they do not trust the results of the 2020 election.[[574]](#cite_note-NPR-poll-577) According to a poll by [Quinnipiac](/wiki/Quinnipiac_University "Quinnipiac University"), 77% of Republicans believe there was widespread voter fraud.[[575]](#cite_note-578)
5. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-died_652-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-died_652-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-died_652-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-died_652-3) Died in office
6. **[^](#cite_ref-653)** Resigned from office
7. **[^](#cite_ref-654)** Comparing seats held immediately preceding and following the general election
8. **[^](#cite_ref-tie1_655-0)** Republican Vice President [Dick Cheney](/wiki/Dick_Cheney "Dick Cheney") provided a [tie-breaking vote](/wiki/List_of_tie-breaking_votes_cast_by_the_vice_president_of_the_United_States "List of tie-breaking votes cast by the vice president of the United States"), initially giving Republicans a majority from [Inauguration Day](/wiki/First_inauguration_of_George_W._Bush "First inauguration of George W. Bush") until [Jim Jeffords](/wiki/Jim_Jeffords "Jim Jeffords") left the Republican Party to caucus with the Democrats on June 6, 2001.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-tie2_656-0)** Democratic Vice President [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris") provided a [tie-breaking vote](/wiki/List_of_tie-breaking_votes_cast_by_the_vice_president_of_the_United_States "List of tie-breaking votes cast by the vice president of the United States"), giving Democrats a majority from [Inauguration Day](/wiki/Inauguration_of_Joe_Biden "Inauguration of Joe Biden") until the end of the [117th Congress](/wiki/117th_Congress "117th Congress").
10. **[^](#cite_ref-660)** Incumbent vice-president [James S. Sherman](/wiki/James_S._Sherman "James S. Sherman") was re-nominated as Taft's running-mate, but died six days before the election. Butler was chosen to receive the Republican vice-presidential votes after the election.

1. **[^](#cite_ref-657)** Lincoln and Johnson were nominated by the [National Union Party](/wiki/National_Union_Party_(United_States) "National Union Party (United States)"), a [wartime](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") coalition of Republicans, [War Democrats](/wiki/War_Democrat "War Democrat"), and [Unconditional Unionists](/wiki/Unconditional_Union_Party "Unconditional Union Party").
2. **[^](#cite_ref-658)** Although Hayes won a majority of votes in the Electoral College, Democrat [Samuel J. Tilden](/wiki/Samuel_J._Tilden "Samuel J. Tilden") won a majority of the popular vote.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-659)** Although Harrison won a majority of votes in the Electoral College, Democrat [Grover Cleveland](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland") won a plurality of the popular vote.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-661)** Taft finished in third place in both the electoral and popular vote, behind [Progressive](/wiki/Progressive_Party_(United_States,_1912) "Progressive Party (United States, 1912)") [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt").
5. **[^](#cite_ref-662)** Although Bush won a majority of votes in the Electoral College, Democrat [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore") won a plurality of the popular vote.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-663)** Although Trump won a majority of votes in the Electoral College, Democrat [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") won a plurality of the popular vote.
